class_schedule.topic: What is Strategy? The Strategic Management Process
learning_objectives: Distinguish strategy from operational effectiveness and explain why the distinction matters for long-run profitability
Describe the strategic management process: mission, external and internal analysis, formulation, implementation, evaluation
Explain the difference between intended, deliberate, emergent, and realized strategy using Mintzberg's framework
Identify the levels of strategy (corporate, business, functional) and the decisions at each level
readings: Porter, 'What is Strategy?' Harvard Business Review (November-December 1996)
Grant, Contemporary Strategy Analysis (11th ed.), Ch. 1 - The Concept of Strategy
Mintzberg, 'Patterns in Strategy Formation,' Management Science (1978)
Rumelt, Good Strategy Bad Strategy (2011), Ch. 1-2 - The Kernel of Good Strategy

lecture_notes.introduction: Strategy is the most misused word in business. Every consulting deck, annual report, and executive email invokes 'strategy' to describe what are often just goals, wishes, or operational improvements. Michael Porter's 1996 article 'What is Strategy?' settled the definition and remains the single most important reading in the field: strategy is the deliberate choice of a different set of activities to deliver a unique mix of value. It is not being the best; it is being different in a way that matters to a specific set of customers. Porter's central point is that operational effectiveness - doing the same things as competitors better - is necessary but not sufficient and tends to be competed away. Sustainable profitability requires strategic positioning, which means making tradeoffs, creating fit among activities, and refusing to do things that conflict with your chosen position. Southwest Airlines is strategic because its entire system (point-to-point routes, single aircraft type, no meals, 15-minute turnarounds, casual culture) fits together in a way that would hurt full-service airlines if they copied any one piece. American Airlines could buy 737s, but it cannot abandon its hub-and-spoke system, loyalty program, and first-class cabins without destroying its current value proposition. That is the nature of strategic fit: activities reinforce each other, and copying one piece without the others is counterproductive. This course will take you from Porter's foundational insights through modern frameworks (VRIO, value chain, Five Forces, blue ocean strategy, disruptive innovation) to the practical challenge of executing strategy in a real organization. You will analyze successful and failed strategies, build your own analytical toolkit, and leave the course able to diagnose why companies win, why they lose, and what to do about it.
key_concepts: Strategy vs. operational effectiveness: operational effectiveness means doing the same activities better (TQM, benchmarking, lean); strategy means doing different activities or the same activities differently - creating a distinctive competitive position
Strategic positioning: the deliberate choice of activities that deliver a unique value proposition to a specific set of customers (needs-based, access-based, or variety-based positioning)
Tradeoffs: strategy requires saying no; companies that try to serve all customers with all needs in all channels end up with average performance (Porter's 'stuck in the middle' critique)
Fit: activities in a strategy must reinforce each other, making the whole system hard to imitate even if individual activities are not novel (Southwest's system of decisions)

Mintzberg's strategy framework: intended strategy (planned), deliberate (intended + realized), emergent (realized without being intended), unrealized (intended but not realized) - real strategy is a mix of deliberate and emergent

Levels of strategy: corporate (which businesses to be in, diversification, M&A), business (how to compete in each business), functional (marketing, operations, HR decisions supporting business strategy)
Strategic management process: analyze (external + internal) -> formulate (mission, objectives, strategy) -> implement (structure, systems, culture, leadership) -> evaluate (performance, adjustment)
Rumelt's kernel of good strategy: diagnosis (what is the challenge?), guiding policy (approach to dealing with the challenge), coherent actions (coordinated steps that execute the guiding policy)

Bad strategy warning signs: fluff (grand language without substance), failure to face challenges, mistaking goals for strategy, bad strategic objectives (impracticable goals that do not address real obstacles)
Competitive advantage: the ability to create more economic value than competitors; measured by profitability differentials sustained over time rather than short-term performance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze Southwest Airlines against a full-service carrier using Porter's activity system view. Map Southwest's activities: point-to-point routes, single aircraft type (737), limited passenger service, short ground times, high aircraft utilization, frequent reliable departures, lean ground crews, automated ticketing, employee ownership. Show how each activity reinforces the others and how copying one activity (e.g., a single aircraft type) without the others destroys value for a hub-and-spoke carrier. Contrast with a failed imitation attempt (Continental Lite, Delta Song) and diagnose why they failed: they kept hub operations, union contracts, and loyalty programs that conflicted with the low-cost model. Introduce Rumelt's framework to distinguish Southwest's strategy (clear diagnosis: short-haul travel is overpriced; guiding policy: deliver frequent reliable cheap flights; coherent actions: an integrated operating system) from bad strategy examples.
setup_requirements: Porter 'What is Strategy?' HBR article (full text)
Case packet: Southwest Airlines activity system diagram
Rumelt Good Strategy Bad Strategy Chapters 1-2 reading notes
Whiteboard or digital canvas for activity mapping exercise

review.value: Doing the same things as competitors but better (OE) gets competed away as best practices diffuse; sustainable advantage requires strategic positioning - choosing different activities or configuring them differently. Toyota's lean production was once OE advantage but has been widely copied; Toyota now depends on other distinctive capabilities to compete

Strategy means saying no. Southwest cannot serve business travelers who want first class, meals, and assigned seats without destroying its cost structure. A firm that tries to be everything to everyone gets stuck in the middle, average at everything and best at nothing

When dozens of activities reinforce each other, imitation requires copying the entire system, which is nearly impossible for an incumbent with existing customer promises and asset commitments. Fit, not individual activities, is what makes strategy durable

Not all realized strategy is planned. Honda's entry into the US motorcycle market succeeded with small bikes even though the deliberate strategy was to push large bikes. Learning, experimentation, and adaptation produce emergent strategy that must be recognized and harvested, not suppressed

class_schedule.topic: The External Environment: PESTEL and Industry Analysis
learning_objectives: Conduct a PESTEL analysis of the macro-environment and identify strategic implications
Distinguish between remote, industry, and competitive environments and their respective analytical frameworks
Identify industry drivers of change using scenario planning techniques
Separate noise from signal in environmental scanning using strategic relevance filters
readings: Grant, Contemporary Strategy Analysis (11th ed.), Ch. 2 - Goals, Values, and Performance
Grant, Contemporary Strategy Analysis (11th ed.), Ch. 3 - Industry Analysis Foundations
Johnson, Scholes & Whittington, Exploring Strategy (12th ed.), Ch. 2 - Macro-Environment Analysis
Schoemaker, 'Scenario Planning: A Tool for Strategic Thinking,' Sloan Management Review (1995)

lecture_notes.introduction: Before you can decide what to do, you must understand the world you are operating in. Strategy cannot be formulated in a vacuum - it depends on political realities, economic cycles, social trends, technological shifts, environmental constraints, and legal frameworks. The PESTEL framework organizes this macro-environmental analysis into six categories so nothing is overlooked. But PESTEL alone is descriptive; strategic analysis requires translating environmental factors into specific implications for your industry and firm. The Ford Motor Company that failed to see the electric vehicle transition coming paid a strategic price; Tesla that saw it early and bet accordingly captured enormous value. The pharmaceutical companies that failed to anticipate the Inflation Reduction Act's Medicare drug price negotiation provisions are now restructuring their pipelines. Netflix saw the broadband transition early and went from mailing DVDs to streaming, while Blockbuster saw the same data and missed it. Environmental scanning is not optional; it is the foundation of all strategy work. This session teaches you to use PESTEL systematically, to identify which environmental factors are strategically relevant for a specific firm (not every factor matters equally), and to use scenario planning to handle genuine uncertainty about the future. Scenario planning, pioneered at Royal Dutch Shell in the 1970s, is the technique that let Shell anticipate the 1973 oil crisis when competitors did not. The goal is not to predict the future - that is impossible - but to be prepared for multiple plausible futures so you can act faster than competitors when the signals emerge.
key_concepts: PESTEL framework: Political (government policy, stability, trade), Economic (growth, inflation, interest rates, unemployment), Social (demographics, cultural norms, lifestyle), Technological (innovation rate, disruption potential, R&D), Environmental (climate, sustainability, ESG), Legal (regulation, antitrust, IP)
Three environmental layers: remote (macro factors affecting all firms), industry (factors affecting all firms in an industry), competitive (rivals, suppliers, customers specific to the firm)
Drivers of change: the small number of factors that will most shape the industry's future; distinguishing drivers from noise is a core analytical skill
Strategic relevance filter: not every environmental factor affects your strategy; filter by (1) magnitude of impact, (2) probability of occurrence, (3) time horizon, (4) ability to respond
Scenario planning: identifying two or three critical uncertainties, constructing divergent plausible futures, stress-testing strategy against each; used by Shell, Unilever, Pentagon
Weak signals vs strong signals: weak signals (early indicators of change) are harder to detect but more valuable strategically; strong signals are obvious but late
Complementors and ecosystems: modern industries depend on complementary products and services (iPhone + App Store); environmental analysis must include ecosystem partners
Geopolitical risk: trade wars (US-China tariffs), sanctions (Russia), export controls (semiconductors), data localization laws (GDPR, PIPL) are now first-order strategy concerns
Regulatory capture and lobbying: firms actively shape their regulatory environment; strategy includes government relations as a deliberate capability
Environmental turbulence and VUCA: volatility, uncertainty, complexity, ambiguity; high-VUCA environments require more frequent strategy review and more flexible commitments
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conduct a live PESTEL analysis of the US automobile industry in 2026. Political: EV subsidies (IRA), UAW contracts, trade policy toward China. Economic: interest rates, consumer spending on durables, used car market. Social: urbanization, changing attitudes toward vehicle ownership (Gen Z ridesharing preference). Technological: battery cost curves, autonomous driving, software-defined vehicles. Environmental: climate regulation, emissions standards, supply chain sustainability. Legal: NHTSA regulation, state-level EV mandates (California CARB), data privacy. Then filter for strategic relevance for Ford vs. Tesla vs. GM. Show how the same environment creates different strategic implications for different firms.
setup_requirements: PESTEL worksheet template
Recent industry data sources: IBISWorld, Statista, Bureau of Labor Statistics
Shell Scenario Planning case (Wack, Harvard Business Review 1985)
News articles from 2025-2026 covering auto industry trends

review.key: PESTEL is a Starting Point, Not the Answer
review.value: A PESTEL analysis that just lists factors is worthless. The analysis must translate factors into strategic implications for a specific firm. A political factor like EV subsidies matters enormously for Tesla, moderately for Ford, and negligibly for a truck parts supplier - the implications differ by firm even though the factor is the same

When you cannot assign probabilities to futures (Will the Fed raise rates? Will China invade Taiwan? Will ChatGPT replace software engineers?), scenario planning is the right tool. You develop 3-4 coherent plausible futures and test your strategy against each, ensuring you are not brittle to any one scenario

Companies that detect weak signals (early indicators of change) gain preparation time that competitors lack. Amazon saw e-commerce emerging in 1994 when bookstores dismissed it. Netflix saw streaming viable in 2007 when Blockbuster still sold late fees. Early detection is a capability worth investing in

review.key: Geopolitics Back at the Center of Strategy
review.value: After three decades of globalization, strategy must once again grapple with geopolitics. Semiconductor firms now think about Taiwan Strait risk. Pharma thinks about Chinese API sourcing. Tech thinks about export controls on AI chips. Political risk cannot be outsourced to a government affairs team

learning_objectives: Apply Porter's Five Forces framework rigorously to diagnose industry attractiveness
Identify the underlying structural drivers of each force and how they determine competitive intensity
Distinguish short-term industry conditions from long-term structural features
Use Five Forces to identify strategic opportunities for improving industry position
readings: Porter, Competitive Strategy (1980), Ch. 1 - The Structural Analysis of Industries
Porter, 'The Five Competitive Forces That Shape Strategy,' Harvard Business Review (January 2008) - updated treatment
Grant, Contemporary Strategy Analysis (11th ed.), Ch. 3 - Industry Analysis
Ghemawat, 'Competition and Business Strategy in Historical Perspective,' Business History Review (2002)

lecture_notes.introduction: Michael Porter's Five Forces framework, introduced in 1979 and expanded in the 1980 book Competitive Strategy, is the single most taught analytical tool in strategic management. It answers the fundamental question: why are some industries more profitable than others? The answer is structural. Airlines have chronically low profitability not because airline executives are incompetent but because the industry's structure - high fixed costs, commodity product, powerful aircraft manufacturers, price-sensitive customers, intense rivalry - produces systematically poor returns on capital. Pharmaceuticals, meanwhile, earn high returns because patents create barriers to entry, FDA regulation limits rivalry, suppliers (raw material providers) have little leverage, and buyers (insurers) face significant switching costs when drugs are patent-protected. Industry structure sets the ceiling on profitability; firm strategy determines where in that range you land. But Five Forces is more than a descriptive tool. Sophisticated strategists use it offensively to identify ways to reshape industry structure in their favor: Apple weakened supplier power over iPhone components by designing chips in-house; Netflix reduced buyer power over content distributors by creating a direct-to-consumer model; Walmart built scale that shifted bargaining power over suppliers. The goal of this session is not to mechanically apply Five Forces but to understand the structural drivers of profitability deeply enough to see opportunities that mechanical application would miss.
key_concepts: Threat of new entrants: economies of scale, capital requirements, switching costs, access to distribution, brand identity, proprietary technology, expected retaliation; high entry barriers protect incumbents and industry profitability

Bargaining power of suppliers: supplier concentration relative to industry, importance of volume to supplier, differentiation of inputs, switching costs, threat of forward integration; powerful suppliers extract value from the industry

Bargaining power of buyers: buyer concentration, purchase volume, switching costs, product importance, buyer information, threat of backward integration; powerful buyers extract value from the industry

Threat of substitutes: relative price-performance of substitutes, switching costs, buyer propensity to substitute; substitutes set a ceiling on industry pricing (cable TV set ceiling for newspaper advertising)
Competitive rivalry: number of competitors, industry growth, fixed costs, exit barriers, differentiation, diversity of competitors; high rivalry erodes industry profitability

Complementors (sixth force by Brandenburger & Nalebuff, 1996): products or services that increase the value of the focal product (apps for iPhone, tires for cars); complementors can act like suppliers (competing for value) or buyers (expanding the pie)

Industry attractiveness vs firm profitability: Five Forces determines industry average profitability; firm position within the industry (cost leader, differentiator, or focused) determines where relative to the industry average a specific firm performs
Structural shifts: industry structure changes over time due to technology, regulation, demographics - Five Forces must be applied dynamically, not as a snapshot

Profit pool analysis: total profits across the value chain may concentrate in specific activities (chip design for semiconductors, platforms for mobile) - analyzing where profit pools exist reveals strategic opportunities
Coopetition: cooperation with competitors (shared standards, industry associations) can improve overall industry profitability and coexist with direct rivalry on distinct dimensions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparative Five Forces analysis of two industries: Airlines vs. Pharmaceuticals, then diagnose why ROIC differs (airlines ~3-5%, pharma ~15-20% over decades). Airlines: low entry barriers post-deregulation, Boeing/Airbus duopoly with high supplier power, commoditized product with high buyer price sensitivity, substitutes (car, train, video conferencing) increasingly competitive, high fixed costs force capacity-based rivalry, high exit barriers (expensive aircraft can only fly). Pharma: high entry barriers (patents, R&D costs, FDA approval), manageable supplier power (diversified inputs), buyer power variable (insurers growing), substitutes limited for patented drugs, rivalry moderate due to differentiation, exit barriers moderate. Then identify reshaping opportunities: a new-entrant airline could try to weaken buyer power by differentiating (JetBlue early positioning), reduce supplier power by flying alternative aircraft (Southwest 737-only), or reduce rivalry by finding a niche with no direct competitor (regional airlines, all-business-class).
setup_requirements: Five Forces worksheet template with structural drivers for each force
Industry profitability data: airline vs pharma ROIC time series from Compustat
Porter HBR 2008 update for current framing
Case materials: selected pages from Competitive Strategy (Porter 1980) Ch. 1

review.value: Industry structure determines average profitability; brilliant management in a bad industry underperforms average management in a good industry. This is why industry selection is a first-order strategic decision - Warren Buffett prefers to be in 'good businesses' rather than try to run bad businesses well

In most industries, one or two forces dominate. In pharmaceuticals, entry barriers (patents, R&D) are everything. In retail, buyer power and supplier power dominate. In airlines, rivalry and supplier power crush returns. Rigorous Five Forces analysis identifies which forces matter most, not equal weighting across five boxes

Five Forces is not just descriptive. Strategists use it to identify how to RESHAPE industry structure: raise entry barriers (patents, scale, brand), reduce supplier power (multi-source, vertical integration), reduce buyer power (differentiation, loyalty), reduce rivalry (industry consolidation, tacit collusion). Apple's A-series chip design weakened Qualcomm's supplier power over iPhone

Industry structure evolves. Newspapers had high entry barriers (capital-intensive printing, distribution) until the internet made distribution nearly free and craigslist destroyed classified ads. Five Forces must be applied as a time series, not a snapshot, and with explicit attention to drivers of change

class_schedule.topic: Internal Analysis: Resources, Capabilities, and VRIO
learning_objectives: Distinguish resources from capabilities and identify both in a specific firm
Apply the VRIO framework (Valuable, Rare, Inimitable, Organized) to evaluate sources of competitive advantage
Identify dynamic capabilities and explain their role in sustaining advantage over time
Connect internal analysis to strategy formulation: matching internal strengths to external opportunities
readings: Barney, 'Firm Resources and Sustained Competitive Advantage,' Journal of Management (1991)
Barney, 'Looking Inside for Competitive Advantage,' Academy of Management Executive (1995)
Grant, Contemporary Strategy Analysis (11th ed.), Ch. 5 - Analyzing Resources and Capabilities
Teece, Pisano & Shuen, 'Dynamic Capabilities and Strategic Management,' Strategic Management Journal (1997)

lecture_notes.introduction: If industry structure sets the ceiling on profitability (Five Forces), internal capabilities determine where within that range a specific firm lands. Why does Toyota outperform General Motors within the same industry structure? Why does Costco outperform Sears even though both are retailers? The answer is found inside the firm: superior resources and capabilities that produce more value or lower cost than rivals. The resource-based view (RBV) of strategy, developed by Jay Barney, Edith Penrose, and others, flipped the emphasis from external positioning to internal assets. The key insight is that not all resources produce advantage - only those that are Valuable, Rare, Inimitable, and Organized to capture (the VRIO test) create sustainable advantage. A resource is Valuable if it exploits opportunities or neutralizes threats. It is Rare if few competitors have it. It is Inimitable if competitors cannot copy or substitute for it at reasonable cost. It is Organized to be captured if the firm's structure, processes, and policies allow it to be deployed. A resource that fails any of these tests fails to produce sustained advantage. Walmart's distribution system is valuable (reduces cost), rare (few retailers have invested similarly), hard to imitate (decades of accumulated data, scale, and learning), and organized through Walmart's systems and culture. Its competitive advantage has endured since the 1980s. This session teaches you to conduct a rigorous internal audit using VRIO and to connect internal analysis to strategy formulation.
key_concepts: Resources: tangible (plants, equipment, capital, location) and intangible (brand, reputation, patents, culture) assets the firm owns or controls; resources are inputs to production
Capabilities: what the firm can DO with its resources; capabilities are the skills, processes, and routines that transform inputs into outputs and customer value

Core competence (Hamel & Prahalad 1990): a capability that (1) provides access to multiple markets, (2) makes a significant contribution to customer-perceived value, (3) is difficult for competitors to imitate; e.g., Honda's engine expertise

VRIO framework: Valuable (exploits opportunity or neutralizes threat), Rare (few rivals have it), Inimitable (hard to copy through physical uniqueness, path dependence, causal ambiguity, or social complexity), Organized (firm is configured to exploit it)

Sources of inimitability: physical uniqueness (mineral rights), path dependence (decades of learning), causal ambiguity (hard to identify which resource creates advantage), social complexity (culture, trust, relationships that cannot be engineered)
Dynamic capabilities (Teece): the firm's ability to sense opportunities, seize them, and reconfigure resources - essential in turbulent environments where static resources quickly become obsolete

Value chain (Porter): primary activities (inbound logistics, operations, outbound logistics, marketing and sales, service) and support activities (firm infrastructure, HR, technology, procurement); each activity is a potential source of advantage

Resource-based view vs positioning view: complementary, not competing - the RBV explains HOW firms achieve advantage (through distinctive resources); positioning view explains WHERE advantages come from in industry terms

Resource heterogeneity and immobility: the assumptions underlying the RBV - firms differ in their resources, and those resources cannot be easily traded in markets, creating persistent performance differences
Identifying capabilities through process mapping: capabilities are embedded in how work gets done, visible through activity-by-activity process analysis rather than on a balance sheet
lecture_notes.demonstration: VRIO analysis of Walmart's distribution system vs. Amazon's fulfillment network. Walmart distribution: Valuable (low-cost retail requires efficient flow); Rare (few rivals invested in comparable scale in the 1980s-2000s); Inimitable (40 years of accumulated learning, data, supplier relationships, geography-specific routing, culture of execution); Organized (Walmart's systems, inventory management, vendor-managed inventory collaborations). Amazon fulfillment network: Valuable (fast delivery is the core promise); Rare (few rivals have comparable scale); Inimitable (complex software, robotics, real-estate footprint, labor systems); Organized (Amazon's operating system, metrics culture). Contrast both with Kmart, which had similar resources (stores, capital) but lacked the capability to use them effectively - the resources existed, but the organization did not capture value from them.
setup_requirements: VRIO analysis worksheet template
Porter value chain template
Comparative financial data: Walmart, Target, Kmart, Amazon ROIC and ROA
Hamel & Prahalad core competence article reading

review.value: Resources are what you HAVE (Boeing's aircraft, Apple's cash, Google's patents); capabilities are what you can DO (Toyota's production system, Apple's design process, Google's search algorithm development). Capabilities are typically more durable sources of advantage because they are harder to copy - resources can often be bought in markets

A resource must pass all four tests to create sustained advantage. Valuable but not rare: competitive parity. Rare but not inimitable: temporary advantage that erodes. Rare and inimitable but not organized: unexploited potential. Organized but the underlying resource is not valuable: wasted effort. Check all four

Many of the most durable advantages come from socially complex resources - culture, relationships, reputation, trust - that competitors literally cannot buy or build on command. Southwest's culture of employee ownership and humor cannot be copied by a Boeing executive who orders a culture change; it evolved over decades

In stable environments, static capabilities produce durable advantage. In turbulent environments (tech, pharma, entertainment), the ability to sense change, seize opportunities, and reconfigure becomes the meta-capability. Amazon is a case study in dynamic capabilities

learning_objectives: Synthesize external and internal analyses using the SWOT framework rigorously
Identify strategic fit between capabilities and opportunities
Avoid the common failure modes of SWOT (laundry lists, unprioritized observations, confirmation bias)
Develop strategy options from SWOT using the TOWS matrix approach
readings: Grant, Contemporary Strategy Analysis (11th ed.), Ch. 1, Section 1.3 - The Strategy Diamond
Hill, Jones & Schilling, Strategic Management: Theory & Cases (13th ed.), Ch. 3 - SWOT Integration
Weihrich, 'The TOWS Matrix: A Tool for Situational Analysis,' Long Range Planning (1982)
Hambrick & Fredrickson, 'Are You Sure You Have a Strategy?' Academy of Management Executive (2001)

lecture_notes.introduction: SWOT (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats) is the most widely used strategic analysis framework - and probably the most widely misused. Done poorly, it produces a laundry list of observations with no strategic insight. Done well, it synthesizes external and internal analysis into actionable strategy options. The critical move is the synthesis step: matching strengths to opportunities, using strengths to neutralize threats, improving weaknesses that block opportunity capture, and avoiding areas where weaknesses and threats combine. Heinz Weihrich's TOWS matrix (1982) formalizes this synthesis by crossing internal factors (strengths, weaknesses) with external factors (opportunities, threats) into four strategic quadrants: SO (strength-opportunity) strategies that aggressively pursue growth, ST (strength-threat) strategies that use strengths to defend against threats, WO (weakness-opportunity) strategies that fix weaknesses to capture opportunities, and WT (weakness-threat) strategies that retreat or defend. This session teaches you to use SWOT as a synthesis tool rather than a checklist. We will examine failure modes: confirmation bias, unprioritized observations, missing strategic implications, and copying generic templates.
key_concepts: SWOT four quadrants: internal factors (Strengths, Weaknesses) and external factors (Opportunities, Threats) - the fundamental structure of strategic situation analysis
Strengths: valuable, rare, inimitable, organized capabilities and resources that produce advantage (drawn from VRIO analysis)
Weaknesses: gaps, deficiencies, vulnerabilities that limit performance or block strategy execution
Opportunities: favorable external trends, unmet market needs, weakening competitors, or industry restructuring that the firm could exploit
Threats: unfavorable trends, new entrants, substitute products, changing regulation, or rising supplier/buyer power

TOWS matrix strategies: SO (aggressive growth using strengths to capture opportunities), WO (improvement to capture opportunities), ST (defensive use of strengths to counter threats), WT (defensive retreat or restructuring)
Strategic fit: the degree to which external opportunities match internal capabilities; high-fit opportunities should be pursued aggressively, low-fit opportunities avoided or delayed

Laundry list failure: listing dozens of factors without prioritization produces information overload and no strategic clarity; SWOT should focus on the 3-5 most strategically relevant factors per quadrant
Confirmation bias in SWOT: managers tend to see what they want to see; rigorous SWOT requires explicit consideration of disconfirming evidence
SWOT as bridge: a good SWOT connects analysis (Five Forces, VRIO, PESTEL) to formulation (generic strategies, strategic choices)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conduct a rigorous SWOT synthesis for Netflix in 2026. Strengths: 260M+ global subscribers, data-driven content selection, original content engine, streaming technology infrastructure, brand, global reach. Weaknesses: dependence on licensed content expiring, content cost inflation, limited advertising capability compared to native ad platforms, saturation in mature markets. Opportunities: ad-supported tier growth, international expansion in Asia and Africa, gaming expansion, live sports entry. Threats: Disney+, HBO Max, Amazon Prime, YouTube, TikTok as substitute for entertainment time, password-sharing crackdown backlash, regulatory risk. TOWS synthesis: SO strategies (use data-driven content selection + global reach to scale ad-supported tier internationally); WO (fix ad capability to capture ad tier opportunity); ST (use brand and original content engine to differentiate against cheaper rivals); WT (reduce licensed content dependency by accelerating originals).
setup_requirements: SWOT and TOWS matrix worksheet templates
Netflix 10-K filing for financial data
Industry news from 2025-2026 for opportunities and threats
Comparison SWOTs from consulting reports and MBA case discussions

review.value: The strategic power of SWOT comes from the TOWS synthesis step - matching internal factors to external factors to generate strategy options. A SWOT that ends with a four-quadrant list has done the easy half
A 30-item SWOT is a failed SWOT. Prioritize to the 3-5 factors in each quadrant that most affect strategy. If everything matters, nothing matters

review.key: Strengths Must Be Strategic, Not Generic
review.value: Every company has 'talented employees' and 'good customer service.' These are not strategic strengths unless they differentiate the firm from competitors. Test every strength with VRIO
Before finalizing a SWOT, explicitly ask: what evidence would disprove each factor? Strategic analysis without steelmanning the alternatives is just confirmation bias dressed up as analysis

learning_objectives: Explain Porter's three generic strategies: cost leadership, differentiation, and focus
Identify the structural requirements, risks, and sustainability conditions for each generic strategy
Diagnose whether a firm is 'stuck in the middle' and the consequences
Apply generic strategy analysis to real-world firms
readings: Porter, Competitive Strategy (1980), Ch. 2 - Generic Competitive Strategies
Porter, Competitive Advantage (1985), Ch. 1-2 - Competitive Advantage and Generic Strategies
Grant, Contemporary Strategy Analysis (11th ed.), Ch. 7 - The Sources and Dimensions of Competitive Advantage
Hill, 'Differentiation vs. Low Cost or Differentiation AND Low Cost,' Academy of Management Review (1988)

lecture_notes.introduction: Once you understand industry structure (external) and your firm's capabilities (internal), the next question is: how will you compete? Porter's generic strategies - cost leadership, differentiation, and focus - provide three coherent ways to achieve above-average industry returns, each requiring different organizational configurations and involving different risks. Cost leadership means being the lowest-cost producer in the industry, capturing value by pricing at or below competitors and earning superior margins through efficiency. Walmart, Costco, Southwest Airlines, and Ryanair are textbook cost leaders. Differentiation means offering a product or service that customers perceive as uniquely valuable and for which they will pay a premium. Apple, BMW, Tiffany, and Starbucks are differentiators. Focus means serving a narrow market segment better than broader competitors - either through cost focus (Dollar General in rural small-town America) or differentiation focus (Rolex in luxury watches, Ferrari in ultra-premium sports cars). Porter's central warning is against being 'stuck in the middle': trying to pursue both low cost and differentiation without the structural requirements for either produces average performance, not above-average. Sears, Kmart, and JCPenney ended up stuck in the middle as Walmart took the cost end and Target took the differentiation end. The generic strategies framework is sometimes criticized as too simple - Toyota has arguably achieved both low cost and quality differentiation, and IKEA combines low cost with a distinctive brand experience. Modern strategy recognizes that 'pure' generic strategies are ideal types; successful firms often combine elements. But the underlying logic - that strategies require coherent choices with tradeoffs, and trying to be all things to all customers is usually fatal - remains central to strategic thinking.
key_concepts: Cost leadership: systematic pursuit of the lowest cost position in the industry through scale economies, learning curve, capacity utilization, process innovation, tight cost control, and efficient value chain activities
Differentiation: offering something uniquely valuable to customers through product features, service, brand, design, distribution, or technology that justifies a price premium

Focus strategy: targeting a narrow segment (geographic, demographic, product-line) and achieving either cost leadership or differentiation within that segment, avoiding direct competition with broad-based rivals
Cost drivers: scale economies, learning curve, capacity utilization, linkages across activities, interrelationships, integration, timing, discretionary policies, location, institutional factors

Differentiation drivers: product features, product performance, linkages among activities, timing of market entry, location, interrelationships with other business units, learning, integration, scale, institutional factors
Stuck in the middle: firms that pursue cost leadership and differentiation without committing to either end up with neither advantage and below-average profitability
Hybrid strategies: Toyota (quality + cost), IKEA (design + cost), Honda (quality + price), Southwest (cost + service) - arguably successful combinations though Porter disputed their sustainability
Risks of cost leadership: technological change obsolescing cost advantage, imitation, proximity to differentiator, inability to respond to premium-segment shifts, erosion of cost advantage over time

Risks of differentiation: cost gap becoming too wide for customers to pay premium, buyer sophistication reducing need for differentiated features, imitation, focus strategies targeting differentiation segments
Strategic commitment: generic strategies require long-term organizational commitment - cost culture, differentiation investments, and focus discipline cannot be switched on and off
lecture_notes.demonstration: Map generic strategies across the US airline industry. Southwest: cost leadership (low fares, single aircraft, point-to-point, no frills). Delta, American, United: differentiation (loyalty programs, first class, lounges, global network) but increasingly cost-pressured. JetBlue: focus differentiation (targeted premium leisure segment with better in-flight experience). Spirit: cost leadership (ultra-low-cost carrier, unbundled fees). Allegiant: focus cost leadership (small markets underserved by majors). Then analyze stuck-in-the-middle: US Airways before the American merger - too expensive to compete with Southwest, not premium enough to compete with Delta. Compare to retail: Walmart (cost), Target (differentiation), Dollar General (focus cost), Whole Foods (focus differentiation), and former stuck-in-the-middle Sears/Kmart.
setup_requirements: Industry position maps for airlines and retail
Porter Competitive Strategy Ch. 2 reading
ROIC comparison data for airlines (Southwest vs. Delta vs. Spirit vs. JetBlue)
Activity system maps for cost leaders vs differentiators

review.value: Generic strategies work because they require coherent organizational choices. A cost leader needs a cost culture, cost-focused IT systems, cost-optimized supply chain, and a workforce comfortable with tight expense control. A differentiator needs marketing investment, R&D capability, premium-segment sales force, and brand investments. Mixing the two produces incoherent choices

review.key: Stuck in the Middle is Usually Fatal
review.value: Being mediocre on cost AND mediocre on differentiation is worse than being great at either one. Customers who want low cost go elsewhere; customers who want differentiation go elsewhere. The stuck-in-the-middle firm loses both segments

review.key: Hybrid Strategies Exist, But Are Hard
review.value: Toyota's combination of low cost and quality was achieved through a specific production system (TPS) that took decades to build and that competitors still struggle to copy. Hybrid strategies require operational capabilities that let the firm defy normal tradeoffs - they are not a license to be average at both
Focus strategies can be very profitable - In-N-Out Burger, Chick-fil-A, Trader Joe's, Ferrari. Serving a narrow segment extraordinarily well often beats serving a broad segment averagely

learning_objectives: Identify the structural sources of cost advantage in specific industries
Distinguish cost drivers from cost-reduction tactics
Analyze how Walmart built and sustained cost leadership for 40+ years
Evaluate the risks and conditions for sustaining cost leadership
readings: Porter, Competitive Advantage (1985), Ch. 3 - Cost Advantage
Ghemawat, 'Sustainable Advantage,' Harvard Business Review (1986)
Grant, Contemporary Strategy Analysis (11th ed.), Ch. 7, Section 7.3 - Cost Advantage
Stalk, Evans & Shulman, 'Competing on Capabilities: The New Rules of Corporate Strategy,' HBR (1992)

lecture_notes.introduction: Cost leadership is often misunderstood as 'cutting costs' - a tactical approach to expense reduction. True cost leadership is structural: it comes from designing the entire value chain to be fundamentally lower cost than rivals, not just running the same value chain more efficiently. Walmart is not cheap because Walmart executives are better at cost-cutting than Sears executives were. Walmart is cheap because Walmart's distribution system, store locations, inventory management, supplier relationships, labor model, and IT systems are designed to produce systematically lower unit costs. Each individual element might be copied, but the integrated system is nearly impossible to replicate. Bruce Henderson's BCG experience curve showed that unit costs decline predictably with cumulative production volume - so scale and early entry create durable cost advantage in many industries. This insight drove the growth-share matrix and the broader emphasis on market share as a source of advantage. But scale is only one of many cost drivers, and the most powerful cost leaders combine multiple drivers into a coherent system. This session teaches you to analyze cost structure systematically, distinguish cost drivers from cost-reduction tactics, and evaluate whether a cost advantage is structural (sustainable) or tactical (vulnerable to imitation).
key_concepts: Scale economies: cost per unit declines as output increases due to spreading fixed costs over more units, specialization, and negotiating leverage with suppliers
Learning curve / experience curve: unit cost declines by 20-30% with each doubling of cumulative volume due to process learning, product redesign, and worker proficiency
Capacity utilization: high utilization spreads fixed costs over more units; underutilized capacity is a major cost disadvantage
Vertical integration: backward integration into suppliers (e.g., Ford owning steel) can reduce costs when supplier margins are high or coordination is critical
Location advantages: lower labor costs, tax incentives, proximity to inputs or customers, transportation cost savings
Linkages within the value chain: activities that coordinate with each other can reduce total cost (e.g., improving quality in operations reduces service costs)
Input cost advantages: long-term contracts, volume discounts, proprietary access to inputs, strategic supplier relationships
Process innovation: novel production methods that reduce cost more than imitators can achieve
Institutional factors: unionization, regulation, local content requirements affect cost structure
Sustainable cost advantage requires multiple reinforcing drivers, not a single cost-reduction initiative
lecture_notes.demonstration: Deep dive on Walmart's cost leadership. 1. Scale: largest retailer in the world; volume purchasing negotiates below-cost prices from suppliers. 2. Supply chain: cross-docking distribution centers, vendor-managed inventory, RFID tracking, dedicated supplier relationships. 3. Real estate: rural and suburban locations with low land and labor costs; hub-and-spoke distribution around distribution centers. 4. Technology: early investment in retail IT, proprietary inventory systems, real-time sales data shared with suppliers. 5. Labor model: non-union, lean staffing, part-time workforce, lower wages and benefits than competitors. 6. Private label: Sam's Choice, Great Value brands capture additional margin. 7. Culture: 'everyday low cost' mindset institutionalized from Sam Walton era. Each driver alone would provide modest advantage; together they produce a structural cost advantage that has persisted since the 1980s despite repeated competitor attempts to match it. Contrast with Kmart, which had similar scale but lacked supply chain, technology, and execution capabilities.
setup_requirements: Walmart 10-K financial data for cost structure analysis
BCG experience curve reference
Porter Competitive Advantage Ch. 3 reading
Comparative SG&A and gross margin data: Walmart, Target, Kmart, Sears

review.value: Structural cost advantage comes from value chain design and is hard to imitate; tactical cost reduction comes from squeezing expenses and is easy to imitate. Cost leaders build structure; imitators cut costs temporarily

review.key: Cost Leadership Is Not Price Leadership
review.value: A firm can be the cost leader without pricing below competitors - it can charge industry-average prices and earn superior margins. Price decisions are independent of cost position, though cost leaders typically pass some savings to customers to build volume

Target took decades to match Walmart's supply chain sophistication. Amazon has spent billions building fulfillment capability that still doesn't match Walmart's in-store unit economics. Structural cost advantages can persist for 20+ years

review.key: Cost Leadership Is Vulnerable to Disruption
review.value: The biggest risk to a cost leader is not imitation but disruption: a new business model (Amazon vs. Walmart in books), a new technology (streaming vs. physical video rental), or a new substitute (ride-sharing vs. car rental) can make the entire cost advantage irrelevant

learning_objectives: Identify the structural sources of differentiation in specific industries
Distinguish meaningful differentiation (customers pay for it) from cosmetic differentiation (customers do not)
Analyze how Apple, BMW, and Starbucks built and sustain differentiation
Evaluate the risks and conditions for sustaining differentiation advantage
readings: Porter, Competitive Advantage (1985), Ch. 4 - Differentiation
Levitt, 'Marketing Success Through Differentiation - of Anything,' HBR (1980)
Grant, Contemporary Strategy Analysis (11th ed.), Ch. 7, Section 7.4 - Differentiation Advantage
Moon, 'Break Free from the Product Life Cycle,' HBR (2005)

lecture_notes.introduction: Differentiation is often confused with product innovation or marketing spin. True differentiation is creating value for customers that rivals cannot match and capturing part of that value as price premium. Apple's differentiation is not just hardware design - it is an integrated ecosystem of hardware, operating system, app store, services, retail stores, and brand that combines to create a customer experience rivals cannot deliver. BMW differentiates through driving dynamics, engineering heritage, and brand positioning reinforced by decades of consistent investment. Starbucks differentiates through the 'third place' experience, barista training, store design, and brand rituals that transform a commodity (coffee) into a premium experience. In each case, the differentiation survives because it is embedded in a system of complementary activities, not a single feature that competitors could copy. The key question for any differentiator is: will customers actually pay for this? Many firms invest in differentiation that customers do not value (extra features, premium packaging, extensive service menus) and are surprised when the price premium fails to materialize. Rigorous differentiation analysis starts with customer value: what do customers actually care about, how much are they willing to pay for it, and can we deliver it at a cost below the price premium? This session teaches you to analyze differentiation drivers, identify meaningful vs cosmetic differentiation, and evaluate the sustainability of differentiation advantages.
key_concepts: Differentiation drivers: product features, quality, performance, reliability, durability, service, customization, brand, image, reputation, distribution channel relationships
Uniqueness cost: the cost of achieving differentiation; differentiation creates advantage only if the price premium exceeds the uniqueness cost
Value chain approach to differentiation: every activity in the value chain can be a source of differentiation; differentiation must be consistent across activities
Signaling quality: when customers cannot directly observe quality, firms use signals (price, advertising, warranty, reputation, celebrity endorsement)
Experience goods vs search goods: experience goods (wine, consulting) cannot be evaluated before purchase, so signaling and reputation matter more
Brand equity: the value of a brand beyond functional product attributes; builds through consistent quality delivery, marketing investment, and customer experience over time
Customer switching costs: once customers are invested in a differentiated product (learning, data, relationships), switching costs reinforce differentiation advantage
Complementary products: ecosystems of complementary products reinforce differentiation (iPhone + App Store, Xbox + games, Kindle + books)
Differentiation vs niche: differentiation is a broad-market strategy (appeal to many customers at premium prices); focus differentiation targets a narrow segment with extreme differentiation
Sustainable differentiation requires ongoing investment: brand, R&D, service, quality must be continually reinforced; static differentiation erodes
lecture_notes.demonstration: Apple's differentiation analysis. Hardware: industrial design, materials, integration (A-series chips, Retina displays). Software: iOS, macOS, interoperability across devices. Services: App Store, iCloud, Apple Music, Apple TV+, Apple Pay. Retail: Apple Stores as brand experience, Genius Bar service. Marketing: product launches, Think Different campaign, celebrity placements. Ecosystem: AirPods, Apple Watch, CarPlay, HomeKit. Each element reinforces the others: high-quality hardware enables iOS performance, iOS enables premium apps, apps lock in customers, services retain customers, retail supports premium positioning, marketing reinforces brand. Result: gross margins of 38-45% in consumer electronics (an industry where most firms earn 5-10%). Compare to Samsung, which has competitive hardware but weaker software integration, services, and ecosystem - Samsung earns premium margins on Galaxy S flagships but cannot match Apple's profitability because its differentiation is less integrated.
setup_requirements: Apple 10-K financial data showing gross margins vs. industry
Porter Competitive Advantage Ch. 4 reading
Value chain templates for differentiation analysis
Comparative brand valuations: Interbrand rankings

review.key: Differentiation Must Be Valued by Customers
review.value: Many firms invest in features customers do not care about. Before committing to a differentiation investment, ask: will customers pay for this? How much? Can we deliver it at a cost below the premium? Differentiation that fails the customer value test is expense, not investment

A single differentiated feature is copyable. Differentiation embedded in a system of reinforcing activities is not. Apple's ecosystem, BMW's engineering heritage, and Starbucks' store experience each involve dozens of coordinated activities that rivals cannot copy piecemeal

review.key: Brand Is a Proxy for Many Things
review.value: Brand equity captures accumulated customer experiences, promises kept, consistency, and trust. It is not marketing spending per se - it is the residue of consistent delivery. Expensive advertising for a product that disappoints customers destroys brand equity faster than it builds it

Mercedes-Benz's quality advantage over mass-market cars has shrunk as Toyota and Hyundai have closed the quality gap. Differentiation requires continual investment - in R&D, brand, service, and innovation - to stay ahead of imitators

class_schedule.topic: Blue Ocean Strategy and Value Innovation
learning_objectives: Distinguish red ocean (competitive) strategy from blue ocean (uncontested market) strategy
Apply the strategy canvas and four actions framework (eliminate, reduce, raise, create)
Analyze how Cirque du Soleil, Yellow Tail Wines, and Nintendo Wii created blue oceans
Evaluate the sustainability of blue ocean advantages and the risks of imitation
readings: Kim & Mauborgne, Blue Ocean Strategy (expanded ed., 2015), Ch. 1-4
Kim & Mauborgne, 'Blue Ocean Strategy,' HBR (October 2004)
Kim & Mauborgne, 'Value Innovation,' HBR (January-February 1997)
Porter, 'What is Strategy?' HBR (1996) - contrasting view

lecture_notes.introduction: Traditional strategy focuses on winning in existing industries - the 'red ocean' where rivals compete for share of an established market. Blue Ocean Strategy, developed by W. Chan Kim and Renee Mauborgne at INSEAD, argues that the most valuable strategic move is to create uncontested market space where competition is irrelevant. Rather than fighting for share in crowded markets, blue ocean firms redefine the product or service to simultaneously increase customer value and reduce cost - breaking the conventional tradeoff between differentiation and low cost. Cirque du Soleil did this in the entertainment industry: traditional circuses competed on animals, star performers, and ticket prices for a declining family audience. Cirque eliminated animal acts, star performers, and three-ring formats (reducing cost) while adding theme, artistic music, refined environment, and theatrical story (increasing value). The result was a new category - theatrical circus - that attracted adults willing to pay premium prices. Cirque grew from Montreal street performers to a global entertainment company worth $1.5 billion. Yellow Tail Wines did the same in wine: eliminating enological complexity and wine vocabulary while making wine approachable, fun, and easy to choose. Nintendo Wii did it in video games: eliminating hardcore gamer graphics arms race while adding motion-sensing accessibility for non-gamers. Each created a blue ocean by reconstructing market boundaries. This session teaches the analytical tools of blue ocean strategy and evaluates the framework's claims and limits.
key_concepts: Red ocean vs blue ocean: red oceans are existing industries with defined boundaries and known competition; blue oceans are uncontested market spaces created by new demand

Value innovation: simultaneously raising buyer value and reducing cost, breaking the conventional tradeoff - achieved by eliminating features that customers do not value and adding features that create new value
Strategy canvas: a visual tool plotting industry competitive factors and relative performance to reveal the value curve; identifies where firms compete and how the blue ocean firm's curve diverges

Four actions framework: Eliminate (which factors can be eliminated that the industry takes for granted?), Reduce (which factors should be reduced below industry standard?), Raise (which factors should be raised above industry standard?), Create (which factors should be created that the industry has never offered?)
Six paths framework: look across alternative industries, strategic groups, buyer groups, complementary offerings, functional-emotional appeal, and time to discover blue ocean opportunities
Reconstructionist view: market boundaries and industry structure are not fixed - firms can reshape them through strategic action rather than taking them as given
Non-customers: blue oceans are often found by targeting non-customers (people not currently served by the industry) rather than fighting for existing customers
Tipping point leadership: executing a blue ocean strategy requires overcoming cognitive, resource, motivational, and political hurdles through focused interventions on key influencers
Blue ocean imitation: first-mover advantages (brand, learning, scale) plus cognitive barriers typically provide 10-15 years of blue ocean before imitators arrive

Strategic coherence test: for a blue ocean to succeed, the value proposition, profit proposition, and people proposition must align - customers want it, the economics work, and employees can deliver it
lecture_notes.demonstration: Apply the four actions framework to create a blue ocean strategy for a traditional industry. Start with Cirque du Soleil analysis: Eliminate (animal shows, star performers, aisle concession sales); Reduce (fun and humor, thrill and danger); Raise (unique venue); Create (theme, refined environment, multiple productions, artistic music and dance). Plot the value curve against Ringling Bros. to show divergence. Then apply the same framework to Southwest Airlines vs. traditional airlines: Eliminate (meal service, seat assignments, hub connections); Reduce (customer service menu, hub-based routing); Raise (frequency of departures, friendliness); Create (nothing new, really - Southwest is more of a cost-leadership differentiator than blue ocean). Then to Tesla vs. traditional automakers: Eliminate (dealerships, internal combustion complexity, fuel stations); Reduce (traditional marketing); Raise (performance, over-the-air updates, charging network); Create (software-defined vehicle, vertical integration with battery factories and charging infrastructure). Discuss which is a genuine blue ocean and which is a stronger positioning move within an existing ocean.
setup_requirements: Blue Ocean Strategy book Chapters 1-4
Strategy canvas template (Excel)
Four actions framework worksheet
Cirque du Soleil and Yellow Tail case materials

review.value: Blue ocean strategy is not about technological innovation per se. Cirque du Soleil used no new technology. Yellow Tail used no new winemaking techniques. The innovation was in value architecture - what the product offered and what it didn't - not in the underlying technology

The most counterintuitive move in blue ocean strategy is eliminating features customers take for granted. Eliminating animals at a circus or seat assignments at an airline seems crazy until you realize these are cost drivers that do not create proportional value

Blue oceans are typically found among non-customers. Cirque targeted adults who would never attend a circus. Nintendo Wii targeted people who did not play video games. Going where competitors don't look is where blue oceans are found

Blue oceans eventually turn red as imitators arrive. The blue ocean firm's advantage period (often 10-15 years) is based on brand, scale, and cognitive barriers, but not permanent. Winners keep innovating to stay ahead of imitation

learning_objectives: Distinguish sustaining innovation from disruptive innovation using Christensen's framework
Identify the characteristics of disruptive innovations: lower performance on established dimensions, new value propositions, new customer segments
Analyze why incumbents with superior resources fail to respond to disruption
Apply the framework to current disruption cases (EVs, fintech, AI)
readings: Christensen, The Innovator's Dilemma (1997), Ch. 1-2
Christensen & Raynor, The Innovator's Solution (2003), Ch. 2
Christensen, Raynor & McDonald, 'What Is Disruptive Innovation?' HBR (December 2015)
Lepore, 'The Disruption Machine,' The New Yorker (June 2014) - critical view

lecture_notes.introduction: Clayton Christensen's 1997 book The Innovator's Dilemma introduced the most influential framework in modern strategy: disruptive innovation theory. The framework explains a puzzle that had baffled strategists: why do well-managed incumbent firms with superior resources, talented employees, and happy customers fail when disruptive new entrants arrive? The answer is that the very practices that make incumbents successful - listening to customers, investing in high-margin products, maximizing returns on existing assets - are the practices that make them vulnerable to disruption. Disruptive innovations start out inferior on the dimensions that mainstream customers care about. They are worse, cheaper, and serve less-demanding or non-existent customer segments that incumbents rationally ignore. But disruptive innovations improve rapidly and eventually meet mainstream customer requirements - at which point they attack the incumbent's core market with a cost and business model the incumbent cannot match. Digital cameras disrupted film. Minicomputers disrupted mainframes. PCs disrupted minicomputers. Streaming disrupted DVD rentals. Tesla is disrupting internal combustion engines. Fintech is disrupting banking. The theory has been enormously influential - and also criticized. Jill Lepore's New Yorker critique argued Christensen's historical examples don't hold up and the theory is more folk wisdom than science. Still, the core insight - that low-end and new-market entrants with different cost structures can undermine seemingly dominant incumbents - remains a powerful lens for strategic analysis. This session teaches you to use the framework carefully, including its limitations.
key_concepts: Sustaining innovation: improvements that help mainstream customers in established markets (better performance, more features, higher quality); incumbents typically win sustaining innovation battles because they have the resources and customer relationships
Disruptive innovation: innovation that creates a new market and value network, eventually disrupting an existing market by displacing established competitors
Low-end disruption: targeting the least profitable, most overserved customers with a cheaper alternative (e.g., steel minimills attacking integrated mills in rebar, then structural steel)

New-market disruption: targeting non-customers who were previously unable to use the product due to cost, skill, or access barriers (e.g., personal computers targeting consumers who couldn't use mainframes)
Performance trajectory: disruptive products improve faster than mainstream needs rise, so the gap between the product and customer requirements closes over time until disruption occurs
Incumbent's dilemma: incumbents fail to respond because disruption targets small markets, low margins, and unattractive customers - all rational reasons for incumbents to dismiss it
Resource allocation processes: incumbents' processes systematically starve disruptive opportunities of resources in favor of sustaining innovation with current customers
Jobs to be done: customers 'hire' products to do specific jobs - disruption often comes from a product that does the job cheaper, simpler, or more conveniently
Christensen's caveats: not every new entrant is disruptive; not every disruption succeeds; the theory describes a specific pattern, not all industry change
Response strategies: acquire disruptors, create separate organizations to pursue disruptive opportunities, cannibalize yourself before rivals do
lecture_notes.demonstration: Apply disruptive innovation theory to three cases. Case 1: Netflix vs Blockbuster. Netflix started as DVD-by-mail (new-market disruption for customers who found Blockbuster stores inconvenient), then pivoted to streaming (low-end disruption on rental fees), then to originals (sustaining innovation vs. Hollywood studios). Blockbuster had the resources to match Netflix but could not cannibalize its store-based revenue model. Case 2: Tesla vs. GM. Tesla started in the high end (Roadster, Model S) with superior performance in some dimensions (acceleration, tech) but inferior in others (charging, range, service network). Is this disruption? Christensen himself debated it - Tesla targeted premium customers, not low-end, which breaks the traditional pattern. But Tesla's vertical integration, direct sales, and software-defined vehicles are creating a new value network that traditional automakers cannot easily match. Case 3: OpenAI / ChatGPT vs. Google Search. ChatGPT is a new way to do information retrieval that is worse at some things (real-time data, exact source citation) but better at conversational explanation and synthesis. Is this disruption of search? Discuss ambiguity. Use these cases to critique the framework's ambiguities.
setup_requirements: The Innovator's Dilemma selected chapters
Tesla vs. traditional auto case data
Netflix historical financials
Lepore critique for balanced discussion

review.value: Incumbents fail not because they are badly managed but because they are well managed. Listening to customers, investing in profitable products, and maximizing ROI on existing assets are all good practices - that make responding to disruption irrational. Until it's too late

Disruption rarely attacks the core of an incumbent's market. It starts with overserved customers, non-customers, or new use cases. Incumbents' blind spot is the edges of their market where the disruptor gains a foothold

A disruptive product starts below mainstream requirements but improves faster than requirements rise. The critical question for a strategist is: when will the gap close? Forecast disruption by tracking performance trajectories, not current performance

Not every new entrant is disruptive (Tesla's initial high-end strategy doesn't fit). Not every disruption succeeds. The framework is a lens, not a law. Use it alongside other frameworks (Five Forces, blue ocean, platforms) rather than as the single truth

learning_objectives: Distinguish platform businesses from linear (pipeline) businesses
Identify direct, indirect, and data network effects and their strategic implications
Analyze how Airbnb, Uber, and Shopify built platform advantages
Evaluate the risks of platform strategy including multi-homing, regulation, and platform envelopment
readings: Parker, Van Alstyne & Choudary, Platform Revolution (2016), Ch. 1-3
Evans & Schmalensee, Matchmakers: The New Economics of Multisided Platforms (2016), Ch. 1-4
Eisenmann, Parker & Van Alstyne, 'Strategies for Two-Sided Markets,' HBR (October 2006)
Cusumano, Gawer & Yoffie, The Business of Platforms (2019), Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Seven of the ten most valuable companies in the world are platform businesses: Apple, Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon, Meta, Tencent, and NVIDIA (now with platform plays). Platforms are fundamentally different from traditional linear businesses because they create value by facilitating interactions between distinct user groups rather than by making and selling products. Uber does not own cars; Airbnb does not own properties; eBay does not own merchandise. Their value comes from network effects: each additional user on one side makes the platform more valuable to users on the other side. A ride-sharing platform with more drivers attracts more riders, which attracts more drivers, and so on in a self-reinforcing cycle. Once a platform achieves critical mass, it becomes extremely difficult to displace because new entrants face a chicken-and-egg problem: drivers will not join without riders, and riders will not join without drivers. This session teaches you to analyze platform economics, identify the different types of network effects, understand how platforms achieve critical mass, and evaluate the strategic moves available to platform incumbents and challengers. We will examine cases of successful platforms (Airbnb, Uber, Shopify) and failed platforms (Google Plus, Microsoft Windows Phone, Quibi) to understand what makes platforms succeed or fail. We will also address the political and regulatory backlash against large platforms - the GDPR, DMA, DSA in Europe; antitrust cases against Apple, Google, and Meta in the US - that now shape platform strategy fundamentally.
key_concepts: Platform vs pipeline: pipelines create value by transforming inputs into outputs (Ford makes cars from steel); platforms create value by facilitating interactions between user groups (Airbnb connects hosts and guests)
Direct (same-side) network effects: value increases as more users on the same side join (Facebook - more friends makes it more valuable); strong in communication platforms
Indirect (cross-side) network effects: value increases as more users on the OTHER side join (Uber - more drivers makes it more valuable for riders); strong in marketplaces and matching platforms
Data network effects: the platform gets smarter as more data flows through it (Google Search - more queries improve ranking algorithms; Waze - more users improve traffic data)
Critical mass: the threshold number of users at which network effects become self-sustaining; below critical mass, the platform struggles to grow; above, it compounds rapidly

Chicken-and-egg problem: early-stage platforms must solve how to attract one side before the other side exists; strategies include subsidizing one side, seeding with supply, marquee users, geographic focus

Multi-homing: users participating on multiple competing platforms simultaneously; reduces platform lock-in and is a major challenge for platforms trying to consolidate markets (drivers on Uber and Lyft both)
Platform envelopment: one platform absorbing another by adding its functionality (Facebook absorbing Messenger, WhatsApp, Instagram, reducing need for standalone messaging apps)
Winner-take-most dynamics: strong network effects tend to produce highly concentrated markets with one or two dominant platforms, not fragmented competition
Platform governance: rules, standards, and incentives that shape user behavior; platforms must balance openness (more users, more innovation) with control (quality, monetization, brand protection)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze Airbnb's platform strategy through its evolution. 2008 launch: chicken-and-egg problem - no hosts, no guests. Solution: Brian Chesky personally photographed properties in NYC to attract first hosts; listed on Craigslist to reach early guests. 2010s growth: direct and cross-side network effects kick in - more hosts attract more guests which attract more hosts. Critical mass achieved in major cities by 2012-2014. Governance: reviews system to manage quality, trust and safety infrastructure, host education, dispute resolution. Monetization: take rate on booking (host and guest fees). Defensibility: network effects (hard for a new entrant to replicate host supply), data advantage (pricing, search, matching), brand. Challenges: regulation (city laws limiting short-term rentals), multi-homing (hosts also list on VRBO, Booking.com), hotel pushback, COVID near-death experience and recovery. Compare to Uber (strong direct network effects, weak multi-homing protection - drivers and riders use both platforms) and Shopify (indirect network effects through the app and developer ecosystem, very strong defensibility once merchants are onboarded).
setup_requirements: Platform Revolution book chapters
Airbnb, Uber, Shopify 10-K filings
Platform business model canvas template
Cross-side and same-side network effect diagrams

review.value: Many would-be platforms fail to achieve network effects. The effects must be designed into the product through user experience, matching quality, trust mechanisms, and incentive structures. Google Plus launched with Google's distribution and failed because it did not engineer the reinforcing loops that make social networks sticky

review.key: Chicken-and-Egg Is Usually the Hardest Problem
review.value: Solving the bootstrap problem - attracting the first users when the platform has nothing to offer them - is where most platform startups fail. Successful platforms use creative launch strategies: subsidy (Uber paid early drivers), marquee users (LinkedIn started with executives), geographic focus (Airbnb's NYC concentration), piggybacking on existing networks (Airbnb's Craigslist exploit)

When users can easily participate on multiple platforms, network effects create less durable advantage. Uber drivers also drive for Lyft. Airbnb hosts also list on VRBO. This multi-homing limits how aggressively platforms can extract value from users who have alternatives

The world's largest platforms now face serious regulatory scrutiny: EU's DMA and DSA, US antitrust cases, China's tech crackdown. Platform strategy must now include government relations, regulatory compliance, and political risk as first-order concerns - not just growth and engagement

class_schedule.topic: Corporate Strategy: Diversification and Vertical Integration
learning_objectives: Distinguish related from unrelated diversification and evaluate their performance records
Apply the three tests for successful diversification (attractiveness, cost-of-entry, better-off)
Analyze vertical integration decisions using transaction cost economics
Evaluate corporate restructuring options including divestitures and spin-offs
readings: Porter, 'From Competitive Advantage to Corporate Strategy,' HBR (May-June 1987)
Grant, Contemporary Strategy Analysis (11th ed.), Ch. 11 - Diversification Strategy
Williamson, 'The Economics of Organization: The Transaction Cost Approach,' American Journal of Sociology (1981)
Collis & Montgomery, 'Creating Corporate Advantage,' HBR (1998)

lecture_notes.introduction: Corporate strategy answers the question: what businesses should the firm be in? While business-level strategy asks how to compete in a single industry, corporate strategy asks whether to diversify, vertically integrate, or divest - and how those choices create value for shareholders. The track record of corporate diversification is poor. Porter's landmark 1987 study of 33 large diversified US firms found that most had divested more than half of their acquisitions, and shareholder returns from diversification moves were on average negative. The basic problem: for diversification to create value, the combined firm must be worth more than the sum of its parts - synergy must exist. But synergies are often illusory, acquisition premiums are usually high, and managing unrelated businesses imposes coordination costs. Berkshire Hathaway, GE under Jack Welch, and Tata Group are exceptions that built value through diversification, but they are rare. More common is the story of AT&T's acquisition of NCR in 1991 ($7.4B write-off by 1995), AOL-Time Warner in 2000 (the largest value destruction in corporate history), or GE's slow unwinding from 2001-2023. Vertical integration is a related corporate-strategy choice. Should Apple design its own chips, or buy from Qualcomm? Should Netflix make its own content, or license from studios? The answer depends on transaction costs, strategic importance, and capability fit. Oliver Williamson's transaction cost economics (Nobel Prize 2009) provides the framework: when asset-specific investments, uncertainty, and frequency are high, vertical integration is more efficient than market contracting.
key_concepts: Related diversification: moving into businesses that share resources or capabilities with existing businesses (P&G across consumer products, Disney across entertainment)
Unrelated diversification: moving into businesses with no operational overlap, relying on financial or managerial capabilities (GE historically, Berkshire Hathaway, conglomerates)

Porter's three essential tests for diversification: (1) attractiveness test - target industry must be structurally attractive, (2) cost-of-entry test - acquisition cost must not eat all future profits, (3) better-off test - the new unit must gain competitive advantage from its link to the corporation, or vice versa
Economies of scope: cost savings from sharing resources across businesses (shared R&D, distribution, brand, procurement)

Core competence and corporate advantage: parent companies create value by leveraging shared capabilities - Honda's engines across motorcycles, cars, generators, marine; Disney's IP across parks, movies, streaming, merchandise
Transaction cost economics: when market contracts fail due to asset specificity, uncertainty, or opportunism, vertical integration becomes more efficient than outsourcing
Asset specificity: investments specialized to a particular relationship (physical, human, site-specific); high asset specificity favors integration over market contracts
Vertical integration risks: loss of scale economies in the integrated activity, reduced flexibility, capability mismatches, conflict with specialists
Divestiture and spin-off: removing underperforming or non-core businesses to unlock value; spin-offs have historically produced superior shareholder returns (GE, Abbott/AbbVie, Kraft/Mondelez)
Conglomerate discount: the market often values diversified firms at less than the sum of their parts, reflecting complexity, governance problems, and capital misallocation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compare two corporate strategies: Disney (successful related diversification) and GE (failed unrelated diversification). Disney: started with animated films, extended into theme parks (1955), television (ABC acquisition), music, retail, consumer products, cruise lines, streaming (Disney+), sports (ESPN). Each business leverages Disney's IP and brand - Mickey Mouse is valuable across movies, parks, merchandise, and streaming simultaneously. Passes all three Porter tests: entertainment industries are attractive, acquisitions (Pixar, Marvel, Lucasfilm, Fox) have been value-accretive, and each new business gained advantage from Disney's IP library. GE: industrial roots in electrical equipment and lighting. Under Welch, expanded into aircraft engines, medical devices, financial services (GE Capital), NBC Universal, appliances, plastics. By 2001, GE Capital alone was larger than most banks and the primary profit driver. After 2008 crisis, GE Capital had to be shrunk under regulatory pressure; the conglomerate came apart. In 2021-2024, GE split into three independent companies (aerospace, healthcare, energy) to unlock trapped value. Key lesson: related diversification with a common operating thread (Disney's IP and brand) beats unrelated diversification that depends on general managerial skill (GE's operating system didn't transfer well).
setup_requirements: Porter 'From Competitive Advantage to Corporate Strategy' HBR article
Disney and GE historical financial data and M&A history
Transaction cost economics primer
Recent corporate spin-off cases and performance data

review.value: The historical evidence is clear: most corporate diversification fails to create value for shareholders. Acquisition premiums are high, synergies are often illusory, and integration is hard. Before recommending diversification, demand rigorous application of Porter's three tests

When diversification works, it usually works because there is a real underlying capability or resource being shared - Disney's IP, Honda's engines, Apple's design. Unrelated diversification justified only by 'managerial skill' usually doesn't work at scale over time

Integrating reduces transaction costs but loses scale economies and flexibility. The decision depends on asset specificity, uncertainty, capability fit, and strategic importance.
