Apple designs chips in-house because supplier dependency threatened differentiation; Apple outsources logistics to Foxconn because specialization offers scale advantages

When a conglomerate discount exists and the parts would be more valuable separately, spin-offs free each business to focus on its own industry, attract industry-specific investors, and be rewarded for operational performance. GE's recent three-way split, Abbott/AbbVie, and Kraft/Mondelez all produced substantial value

learning_objectives: Apply the McKinsey 7S framework to align organizational elements with strategy
Identify the structural choices (functional, divisional, matrix) that best support different strategies
Analyze how culture enables or blocks strategy execution
Distinguish structural, behavioral, and cognitive barriers to implementation
readings: Peters & Waterman, In Search of Excellence (1982), introducing 7S framework
Galbraith, 'Organization Design: An Information Processing View,' Interfaces (1974)
Kaplan & Norton, 'Using the Balanced Scorecard as a Strategic Management System,' HBR (January 2007)
Kotter, 'Leading Change: Why Transformation Efforts Fail,' HBR (March-April 1995)

lecture_notes.introduction: The gap between strategy formulation and strategy implementation is where most corporate strategy dies. A brilliant strategy that cannot be executed is worth nothing. Research by consulting firms including McKinsey, Bain, and BCG consistently shows that 60-70% of strategies fail to achieve their intended outcomes - and the failure is almost always in execution, not formulation. Why? Because strategy requires changing how thousands of people work, and organizations resist change. The structure that worked for the old strategy doesn't support the new one. The reward systems incentivize old behaviors. The culture honors old heroes. The information systems track old metrics. Unless all these elements are realigned with the new strategy, execution falters. The McKinsey 7S framework, introduced in 1980 by Richard Pascale, Anthony Athos, and Tom Peters at McKinsey, captures seven interdependent elements that must align for strategy execution: Strategy, Structure, Systems (hard elements), and Style, Staff, Skills, Shared Values (soft elements). The framework's key insight is that changing strategy without changing the other six elements is futile - they will pull the organization back to its old patterns. This session teaches you to diagnose alignment problems and to design implementation programs that address all seven elements. We'll examine successful transformations (Satya Nadella at Microsoft 2014-present, Alan Mulally at Ford 2006-2014) and failures (JC Penney under Ron Johnson 2011-2013) to understand what works and what doesn't.
key_concepts: 7S framework: Strategy, Structure, Systems, Style (leadership), Staff (people), Skills (capabilities), Shared Values (culture) - seven interdependent elements that must align for execution

Structure choices: functional (specialization efficiency, poor cross-functional coordination), divisional (profit accountability, potential duplication), matrix (dual reporting for complex decisions, complexity and conflict)
Form follows strategy (Chandler 1962): organizational structure should be designed to support strategy; structural choices that worked for past strategies must evolve with new strategies
Systems: budgeting, performance management, IT, reward systems, decision rights - must be designed to reinforce the desired strategic behaviors

Culture as strategy enabler or blocker: culture is 'the way we do things around here' - it defines acceptable behavior more powerfully than policies do. Culture mismatches kill acquisitions and strategic pivots
Balanced Scorecard (Kaplan & Norton 1996): performance measurement across four perspectives - financial, customer, internal processes, learning and growth - linked to strategic objectives
Change management: Kotter's eight steps - establish urgency, form coalition, create vision, communicate, empower, generate short-term wins, consolidate gains, anchor in culture
Alignment diagnostics: regularly test whether each 7S element supports the current strategy or reflects the previous strategy
Resistance sources: loss aversion, vested interests, cognitive bias, capacity constraints, inadequate communication

Structural barriers vs behavioral barriers: sometimes the structure literally blocks the strategy (e.g., siloed functional reporting for a customer-centric strategy); sometimes the structure is fine but behavior is stuck
lecture_notes.demonstration: Contrast two CEO-led transformations. Satya Nadella at Microsoft (2014-present): inherited a declining, Windows-centric firm with Ballmer-era stack-ranking culture. New strategy: cloud-first, cross-platform (iOS and Android, not just Windows), developer-focused, growth mindset. Applied 7S: Structure (reorg to move from Windows silo to cloud-first, eliminated stack ranking); Systems (new performance reviews, metrics on Azure growth, cloud-credit compensation); Style (Nadella's collaborative leadership replacing Ballmer's combative style); Staff (hired cloud executives from Amazon and elsewhere); Skills (upskilling for cloud, AI, open source); Shared Values ('growth mindset' from Carol Dweck's research replaced know-it-all culture). Result: Microsoft market cap grew from $300B to over $3T. JC Penney under Ron Johnson (2011-2013): former Apple Retail chief hired to transform JC Penney from promotional, middle-market, older-customer store to everyday-low-price store with boutique shop-in-shops targeting younger shoppers. Changed Strategy dramatically without aligning other 7S elements: kept old workforce, old IT systems, old supplier relationships. Eliminated promotional pricing overnight, alienating core customers. Store-within-store concept required capital and execution capability the organization didn't have. Within 17 months, Johnson was fired, sales had collapsed 25%, and JC Penney eventually went bankrupt. Lesson: changing strategy without changing the system around it is catastrophic.
setup_requirements: McKinsey 7S framework diagram and worksheet
Microsoft transformation case materials (Nadella Hit Refresh book excerpts)
JC Penney Ron Johnson case study
Kotter's 8-step change process reading

review.key: Formulation Is Easy; Execution Is Hard
review.value: Most strategies are not badly designed; they are badly implemented. The execution gap is where value is created or destroyed. Great strategists spend more time on execution than on analysis

The 7S framework's central insight is that elements reinforce or undermine each other. Change strategy without changing structure: structure drags strategy back. Change strategy without changing reward systems: people do what they are paid for, not what the strategy requires. All seven must align

Peter Drucker's aphorism 'culture eats strategy for breakfast' is now a consulting cliche, but it's true. When a new strategy conflicts with the existing culture, culture usually wins. Successful transformations require explicit culture work, not just new org charts

Kotter's research shows that change efforts stall after 12-18 months without visible early wins. Implementation programs must design for short-term successes that build momentum and silence critics, not just long-term transformations

learning_objectives: Distinguish strategic leadership from operational management
Identify cognitive biases that systematically distort strategic decisions
Apply techniques for improving strategic decision quality (red teams, pre-mortems, decision hygiene)
Evaluate the role of vision, values, and ethical leadership in strategy
readings: Kahneman, Thinking, Fast and Slow (2011), Ch. 19-24 on decision biases
Finkelstein, Why Smart Executives Fail (2003), selected chapters
Sutherland, 'How to Ensure You're Making the Right Decision,' HBR Analytics (2019)
Lovallo & Kahneman, 'Delusions of Success: How Optimism Undermines Executives' Decisions,' HBR (July 2003)

lecture_notes.introduction: Strategy is not just analytical work; it is leadership work. Even the most rigorous strategic analysis must be converted into action by leaders who inspire, decide, and commit. Strategic leadership - shaping organizational direction, allocating resources, building capabilities, and managing stakeholders - is a distinct skill from operational management. The best strategists are not just good analysts; they are good decision-makers under uncertainty, good communicators of direction, and good judges of when to commit and when to pivot. This session focuses on two threads: decision quality under uncertainty and the role of leadership in strategy execution. Daniel Kahneman's work on cognitive biases (Nobel 2002) documented how even experienced executives systematically make poor strategic decisions due to overconfidence, anchoring, availability bias, and sunk cost fallacies. His collaboration with Lovallo showed that most business forecasts are 'delusional' - systematically overoptimistic and underestimating competition. Understanding these biases is not enough to eliminate them, but structured decision processes (pre-mortems, red teams, outside view, decision hygiene) can substantially improve decision quality. We will also discuss the role of vision and values in strategic leadership. Firms with clear, motivating visions (Microsoft's 'a PC on every desk,' Tesla's 'accelerate the transition to sustainable energy,' Patagonia's 'save our home planet') attract talent, motivate employees, and maintain strategic focus during turbulent times. Firms with vague or cynical visions drift. Finally, we'll discuss ethical leadership and the tension between shareholder primacy (Milton Friedman) and stakeholder capitalism (Edward Freeman, the Business Roundtable 2019 statement).
key_concepts: Strategic leadership: the ability to anticipate, envision, maintain flexibility, and empower others to create strategic change

Cognitive biases in strategic decisions: overconfidence (overestimating own ability and underestimating rivals), anchoring (initial numbers bias subsequent estimates), availability (recent or memorable events overweighted), sunk cost fallacy (continuing to invest because of past commitment rather than future value), confirmation bias
Planning fallacy: systematic underestimation of project cost and duration; executives plan based on best-case scenarios rather than reference-class forecasting
Outside view / reference class forecasting (Kahneman): when forecasting a decision, look at outcomes of similar past decisions rather than building from inside-the-project estimates
Pre-mortem (Klein): before committing to a decision, imagine it has failed and work backward to identify what went wrong; surfaces risks that cheerleading meetings miss
Red team analysis: assigning a subgroup to argue against a proposed decision, forcing rigorous examination of assumptions and alternatives

Decision hygiene: structured processes for reducing bias - separating evidence gathering from conclusion drawing, requiring independent judgments before group discussion, explicitly considering alternatives
Vision statements: a clear, compelling picture of the future state the firm is pursuing; enables alignment and decision-making at scale without constant direction

Stakeholder vs shareholder perspectives: Friedman (1970) - firms should maximize shareholder value within legal and ethical constraints; Freeman (1984) stakeholder theory - firms should create value for employees, customers, suppliers, community, and environment, not just shareholders
Business Roundtable 2019 Statement: 181 CEOs signed a statement endorsing stakeholder capitalism, though academic research shows little behavioral change followed
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conduct a pre-mortem on Ford's 2021 announcement of $30B investment in EV production by 2030. Imagine it's 2030 and Ford's EV strategy has failed. Work backward: What went wrong? Possible pre-mortem findings: (1) Battery technology shifted faster than expected, making Ford's 2022-2024 battery investments obsolete; (2) Chinese competition (BYD) entered US market and undercut Ford EV pricing; (3) IRA subsidies were reduced after 2024 election; (4) UAW contracts made Ford's EV labor costs uncompetitive vs. Tesla; (5) Consumer preference shifted to PHEVs over BEVs due to charging infrastructure limits; (6) Ford's software capabilities (infotainment, OTA updates) lagged Tesla and Chinese competitors; (7) CEO change in 2025 led to strategic vacillation. Now use the pre-mortem findings to adjust the strategy: hedge with PHEV investment, accelerate software hiring, lobby for subsidy continuity, negotiate flexible UAW terms. Compare this to what Ford actually announced - and discuss how pre-mortem analysis would have changed the investment profile. Then examine how Boeing's 737 MAX decisions illustrate biases (overconfidence in MCAS, confirmation bias ignoring test data, time pressure leading to shortcuts) and how a red team might have caught them before certification.
setup_requirements: Kahneman Thinking Fast and Slow selected chapters
Pre-mortem worksheet
Ford EV strategy announcement materials
Boeing 737 MAX case materials (for bias discussion)

review.value: Strategic decision errors are not random noise; they are systematic. Executives consistently overestimate their firm's capabilities, underestimate competitors, anchor on initial estimates, and ignore disconfirming evidence. Structured decision processes reduce these biases; intuition does not

After a decision fails, post-mortems identify what went wrong. Pre-mortems imagine the failure BEFORE the decision and use that imagined failure to improve the plan. This simple technique catches risks that optimistic planning meetings systematically miss

Large organizations cannot be run by constant direction from the top. Clear vision enables thousands of employees to make decisions aligned with strategy without waiting for approval. Microsoft's 'PC on every desk' guided engineering decisions in the 1980s-90s without needing top-down orders

Ethical lapses create strategic risk - Wells Fargo cross-selling scandal, Volkswagen dieselgate, Boeing 737 MAX. Stakeholder trust is a strategic asset that takes decades to build and minutes to destroy. Leaders who treat ethics as compliance theater miss the strategic dimension

learning_objectives: Analyze the challenges of leading strategic change in established organizations
Integrate frameworks from the course into a holistic strategic diagnosis
Apply strategy frameworks to evolving contexts (AI, climate, geopolitics)
Prepare for case-based strategic analysis in the final project
readings: Grant, Contemporary Strategy Analysis (11th ed.), Ch. 17 - Current Trends in Strategic Management
Rumelt, Good Strategy Bad Strategy (2011), final chapters on strategic thinking
Martin, 'The Big Lie of Strategic Planning,' HBR (January-February 2014)
Collis & Rukstad, 'Can You Say What Your Strategy Is?' HBR (April 2008)

lecture_notes.introduction: Strategy is not a plan that survives contact with reality unchanged. It is a continual process of diagnosis, commitment, execution, and adjustment in the face of an environment that refuses to stand still. This final session synthesizes the course into an integrated view of strategic management and addresses the most challenging strategic topic of all: leading change in organizations that resist it. The best strategy is worthless if an organization cannot execute it, and execution requires change, which organizations resist. We will revisit Rumelt's kernel of good strategy (diagnosis, guiding policy, coherent actions) as the integrating framework for all the analytical tools covered: Five Forces, VRIO, PESTEL, SWOT, generic strategies, blue ocean, disruptive innovation, platforms, 7S, decision biases. Each is a tool for a specific diagnostic or design question. The master strategist knows which tool to use when and how to integrate findings into a coherent strategy. We will also discuss the future of strategic management in a world reshaped by AI, climate change, geopolitical fragmentation, and demographic shifts. What does strategic management look like when AI can generate strategic analysis in seconds? When climate risk is a first-order financial risk? When geopolitics is back at the center of global business? When talent is scarce in aging economies and abundant in young ones? The frameworks in this course will still apply, but their application must evolve with the context. Finally, we will preview the final project and capstone case analysis to prepare students for synthesizing the course content into an integrated strategic analysis.
key_concepts: Strategic change: modifying strategy, structure, processes, and culture in response to new challenges or opportunities
Organizational inertia: the tendency of organizations to continue doing what they have always done due to vested interests, cognitive anchoring, and structural commitments
Kernel of good strategy (Rumelt): diagnosis (what is the challenge?), guiding policy (approach to the challenge), coherent actions (coordinated steps)
Strategy statement (Collis & Rukstad): a 35-word statement defining the firm's strategy - objective (ends), scope (domain), advantage (means of winning)
Emergent vs deliberate strategy revisited: real strategy is a mix of deliberate planning and learning from execution; rigid plans fail, pure emergence leads nowhere

Strategic renewal: periodically reinventing strategy as environments change; successful firms do this proactively (Microsoft 2014, IBM 1993, Netflix 2007), failing firms do it reactively (Kodak, Blockbuster)
Ambidexterity: simultaneously exploiting current advantages and exploring new opportunities; separating the two into different organizational units (Alphabet's X labs, GE's Predix failure)
Strategy for digital transformation: integrating digital technology into every aspect of business operations; requires new capabilities, not just new technology

Strategy for climate transition: climate risk is now material financial risk (physical, transition, liability); net zero commitments, scope 1/2/3 emissions, climate-related financial disclosures (TCFD)

Strategy for AI era: generative AI is the biggest technology shift since the internet; reshapes knowledge work, customer service, content creation, software development, requiring new strategic thinking
lecture_notes.demonstration: Integrated strategy diagnosis of a firm using all course frameworks. Select Microsoft in 2014 (when Satya Nadella became CEO) as the case. PESTEL: mobile shift, cloud emergence, consumerization of IT. Five Forces: intense rivalry with Apple, Google, Amazon; powerful buyers as enterprises standardized; substitutes (Google Apps, Linux, web apps) eroding Windows moat. VRIO: Microsoft Office (valuable, rare, imitable through cloud substitutes, organized but threatened); enterprise sales force (valuable, rare, hard to imitate, organized - a durable advantage); Windows (valuable in legacy PCs, declining in relevance); Azure (emerging, potentially rare and valuable). SWOT synthesis: Microsoft has strengths in enterprise relationships and cloud capability, opportunities in AI and cloud transformation, weaknesses in mobile and consumer cloud, threats from Google and Amazon. Generic strategy: differentiation through enterprise integration and trusted brand. Blue ocean: potential in cross-platform productivity (Office on iOS/Android) previously blocked by Windows-centric strategy. Disruptive innovation: SaaS was disrupting installed software, Microsoft needed to cannibalize Office licensing with Office 365 subscriptions. Platform: Azure as platform for developers and enterprises. 7S transformation: Nadella's 7S changes enabled the new strategy. Result: Microsoft's market cap grew from $300B to over $3T in a decade. This analysis uses every framework from the course and shows how they integrate into a coherent strategic diagnosis.
setup_requirements: Course framework integration map
Microsoft under Nadella case materials
Capstone project specification
Strategy statement template (Collis & Rukstad 35-word format)

review.value: The value of strategic management education comes from integrating frameworks into a coherent diagnosis, not mechanical application of individual tools. A strategic analysis that uses Five Forces, VRIO, and SWOT in isolation is three parallel reports; an integrated analysis uses each tool to answer specific questions that feed into a unified diagnosis

Rumelt's insight is that most strategies fail because they skip the diagnosis step. Knowing what the challenge is matters more than generating solutions. A clear diagnosis ('our cost structure is too high because of legacy contracts') focuses action; a vague problem statement ('we need to be more innovative') produces fluff

review.key: Change Is Always Harder Than It Looks
review.value: Every strategic change runs into organizational resistance. Plan for resistance, communicate relentlessly, produce quick wins, and recognize that the sociological work (people, culture, politics) is as important as the analytical work

review.key: Strategy Is Ongoing, Not an Annual Event
review.value: The old model of annual strategic planning with a three-year plan does not work in fast-moving environments. Strategy must be continually tested against reality and adjusted. The best firms review strategy quarterly or continuously, not once a year at an offsite

quizzes.topic: Strategy Foundations, External Analysis, and Industry Structure

questions.question: Porter's 1996 article 'What is Strategy?' argues that operational effectiveness is not strategy. Using a specific example from the airline industry or retail industry, explain the difference and why it matters for long-run profitability.
questions.answer:
Operational effectiveness (OE) means performing similar activities better than rivals - being more efficient, faster, higher quality. Strategy means performing DIFFERENT activities or the same activities in a DIFFERENT way - choosing a distinctive competitive position.

Retail example: Walmart and Target both pursue retail distribution of consumer goods, but their strategies differ. Walmart's strategy is cost leadership through an integrated system: rural locations, massive distribution centers with cross-docking, vendor-managed inventory, private-label brands, lean store labor, and minimal merchandising investment. Target's strategy is 'cheap chic' differentiation: suburban locations, designer partnerships (Michael Graves, Isaac Mizrahi), higher-quality private labels, stronger apparel and home goods mix, and a more experiential store environment. These are different strategies with different activity systems.

If Walmart and Target both pursued operational effectiveness alone - trying to run their current activity systems more efficiently - they would compete head-to-head on execution. Eventually one would win, but more likely both would see margins compressed as best practices diffused.

Instead, both have distinctive strategies that tap different customer segments. Walmart serves price-sensitive customers who want a reliable low price on essentials; Target serves middle-income customers who want design and experience for a modest premium. Each has sustained profitability despite being in the same industry because their strategies are different, not just their operations.

The problem with pursuing only OE is that best practices diffuse. When everyone in an industry adopts the same lean supply chain, the same analytics tools, the same customer service standards, the performance differences that those innovations temporarily created are competed away. OE is necessary but not sufficient - you must also have a distinctive position that makes your firm different, not just better.

The airline industry is the best case for why OE-only strategies fail. Every major airline has pursued OE aggressively: fuel-efficient aircraft, yield management software, lean operations, loyalty programs. But none of these created durable advantage because all competitors adopted them. The airlines with distinctive strategies - Southwest (cost leadership through point-to-point), JetBlue (differentiation through in-flight experience), Allegiant (focus on underserved small markets) - have outperformed the hub-and-spoke majors that pursued OE without strategic differentiation. American, Delta, and United all have similar cost structures, similar route networks, and similar service levels, and their ROIC is chronically below cost of capital.

rubric.key: Correct distinction between OE (doing same things better) and strategy (doing different things)
Specific industry example with different activity systems identified
Explanation of why OE-only competition erodes performance over time

questions.question: Apply Porter's Five Forces to the US ride-sharing industry (Uber, Lyft, etc.) circa 2026. Rate each force as high/medium/low and explain which forces most shape industry profitability.
questions.answer:
Five Forces Analysis: US Ride-Sharing Industry (2026)

1. Threat of New Entrants: MEDIUM-LOW
- Technology development cost is modest relative to capital requirements of building driver supply.
- Strong network effects (more drivers = shorter wait times = more riders = more drivers) protect incumbents.
- Brand recognition for Uber and Lyft is high; new entrants face customer acquisition costs.
- Regulatory complexity varies by city, creating some entry friction.
- Counter: waymo and autonomous vehicles could bypass these barriers if they work.

2. Bargaining Power of Suppliers (Drivers): MEDIUM-HIGH (rising)
- Drivers are independent contractors with multi-homing (drive for Uber AND Lyft), which reduces platform lock-in.
- Driver supply is concentrated in cities where demand is high, giving drivers leverage during peaks.
- California AB5 and similar laws have pushed toward driver reclassification as employees in some jurisdictions.
- Gas prices, vehicle maintenance, and insurance costs affect driver economics, forcing platforms to raise pay to maintain supply.
- Drivers have successfully organized (though not unionized) and pushed for pay transparency and guarantees.

3. Bargaining Power of Buyers (Riders): MEDIUM
- Riders can easily switch between Uber and Lyft with minimal switching cost (both apps installed).
- Price transparency is high (riders see both apps' prices before booking).
- Substitutes exist (public transit, driving, walking, biking) for many trip types.
- Riders have no brand loyalty - they use whichever app offers a better price or ETA for a given trip.

4. Threat of Substitutes: HIGH
- Traditional taxi, public transit, ride-sharing alternatives (Via, HopSkipDrive for specific segments), personal car ownership.
- For short trips, bikes, scooters, and walking substitute.
- For long trips, personal cars, trains, flights.
- Work-from-home trends reduce commuting demand.
- Autonomous vehicles from Waymo, Tesla, Cruise represent major substitute threat.

5. Competitive Rivalry: HIGH
- Uber and Lyft are direct rivals in most US markets with no differentiation except convenience.
- Price wars and subsidies to both drivers and riders have been common.
- Low switching costs for both drivers and riders intensify rivalry.
- Uber has pursued diversification (Uber Eats, freight) partly to escape the core rivalry.

Which forces dominate profitability:

The industry's chronic unprofitability is driven primarily by:
1. Supplier (driver) power rising as drivers organize and regulators push reclassification - squeezes margins.
2. High rivalry between Uber and Lyft - erodes margins through subsidies and competitive pricing.
3. Multi-homing on both sides - limits the lock-in that network effects would otherwise provide.

The industry's above-industry-cost-of-capital profitability depends primarily on:
1. Geographic concentration limits that create local network effects.
2. Incumbent brand and app ubiquity reducing new entrant probability.
3. Potential for differentiation into adjacent categories (delivery, freight).

Strategic implications: Uber and Lyft cannot escape the fundamental structural constraints through operational improvements. Sustainable profitability requires either (a) consolidation (unlikely given antitrust scrutiny), (b) differentiation into adjacent services (Uber Eats pursues this), or (c) radical cost reduction through autonomous vehicles (the ultimate game-changer if it works). Pure ride-sharing will remain a low-margin, contested market.

rubric.key: Correct application of all five forces with defensible ratings
Specific structural drivers identified for each force
Synthesis identifying which forces most affect profitability with strategic implications

questions.question: Explain the difference between VRIO resources and basic resources, and give one specific example of a VRIO-compliant capability at Toyota, Amazon, or Disney.
questions.answer:
Basic resources are inputs that any firm in an industry can access (capital, standard technology, commodity labor, publicly available knowledge). Basic resources do not create competitive advantage because any competitor can acquire them in factor markets.

VRIO resources, by contrast, meet four tests that together create sustained competitive advantage:
- Valuable: exploits opportunities or neutralizes threats in a way that increases customer value or reduces firm cost
- Rare: few or no competitors possess the resource
- Inimitable: competitors cannot easily acquire or substitute for the resource due to physical uniqueness, path dependence, causal ambiguity, or social complexity
- Organized to capture value: the firm's structure, processes, policies, and culture allow the resource to be deployed effectively

Example: Toyota Production System (TPS) as a VRIO-compliant capability.

Valuable: TPS enables Toyota to produce high-quality vehicles at lower cost than most competitors. The system combines just-in-time inventory, jidoka (automation with a human touch), kaizen (continuous improvement), pull production (kanban), and worker empowerment to stop the line. It produces vehicles with fewer defects, lower inventory costs, faster changeover times, and more flexibility than traditional mass production. J.D. Power initial quality studies consistently rank Toyota brands at or near the top.

Rare: While the basic concepts of TPS have been published extensively (James Womack's 'The Machine That Changed the World' made lean production famous in 1990), the full capability is rare. Few automakers - even decades after learning about TPS - have replicated its full effectiveness. Many tried: GM's NUMMI joint venture with Toyota, Ford's attempts to copy lean in the 1990s, Volkswagen's production system. Most achieved partial success but not Toyota's level of integration and consistency.

Inimitable: TPS is extraordinarily hard to copy because it depends on causal ambiguity (which elements produce the results?), path dependence (decades of evolutionary learning), social complexity (a specific culture of worker empowerment, supplier collaboration, and continuous improvement), and tacit knowledge (practices encoded in people, not manuals). GM spent over a decade studying TPS through the NUMMI joint venture and still failed to replicate it across GM plants because the GM organizational culture, labor relations, and management approach were incompatible.

Organized: Toyota has been organized to exploit TPS since Taiichi Ohno developed it in the 1950s-60s. The organizational structure, HR practices (long-term employment, skill investment), supplier relationships (keiretsu), reward systems, and leadership model all reinforce TPS. Toyota's current challenges (quality recalls, software gaps vs. Tesla) come from areas where the traditional TPS strengths are less relevant, not from organizational failure to capture TPS value.

Result: TPS has produced sustained advantage for Toyota for 50+ years in an industry where competitors have nominally understood it for 35 years. This is strong evidence of a VRIO-compliant resource - one so hard to copy that explicit competitor imitation attempts have mostly failed.

rubric.key: Correct distinction between basic resources and VRIO resources
All four VRIO tests applied rigorously to the chosen example
Specific evidence of sustained advantage and failed imitation attempts

quizzes.topic: Business-Level Strategy: Generic Strategies and Positioning

questions.question: Porter argues that firms must choose between cost leadership and differentiation, warning against being 'stuck in the middle.' Yet Toyota appears to combine high quality (differentiation) with low cost. (a) Is Toyota stuck in the middle? (b) If not, what allows Toyota to defy Porter's warning? (c) Can any firm copy Toyota's approach?
questions.answer:
(a) Toyota is NOT stuck in the middle. Porter's 'stuck in the middle' warning applies to firms that achieve neither a cost advantage nor a clear differentiation advantage - they end up with average costs and average prices, unable to compete with cost leaders on price or with differentiators on value. Toyota has both a genuine cost advantage (consistently lower unit cost than GM, Ford, or VW in comparable vehicle segments) AND a genuine differentiation advantage (higher quality and reliability than most mass-market rivals). Toyota does not have lower quality than BMW or Mercedes, but for its target market (mainstream mass-market vehicles), it has both cost and quality leadership simultaneously.

(b) Toyota can defy Porter's warning because of the Toyota Production System (TPS), a specific operational capability that breaks the conventional cost-quality tradeoff. Traditional mass production assumed that higher quality required more inspection, slower throughput, and higher labor costs - making cost leadership and quality leadership inherently incompatible. TPS invalidated this assumption through several innovations:

1. Jidoka (build quality in, not inspect it): workers stop the line when they see a defect, preventing bad parts from moving forward and requiring fixes before continuing. This reduces rework, scrap, and warranty costs while improving quality.

2. Just-in-time inventory: reducing work-in-process inventory to nearly zero forces quality problems to surface immediately (you cannot hide defects in a buffer of work-in-process). This improves quality AND reduces inventory carrying costs.

3. Kaizen (continuous improvement): small daily improvements by front-line workers accumulate into large productivity gains over time. This reduces costs while maintaining quality.

4. Supplier partnerships (keiretsu): long-term supplier relationships enable collaboration on cost and quality improvements that arms-length transactional relationships cannot achieve.

5. Worker empowerment: workers who see problems have authority to stop production and drive improvements. This engagement produces both better quality and better cost.

The result is that TPS shifts the entire cost-quality frontier outward: Toyota can produce at a cost-quality combination that other manufacturers using traditional methods literally cannot reach. This is not 'stuck in the middle' - it is operating on a different production possibilities frontier.

However, note that Porter himself addressed this in later work. His critique of firms that 'try to do both' without distinctive operational systems still holds. Toyota's exception proves the rule: achieving both cost and differentiation advantage requires a specific operational capability that generates a new frontier, not a mediocre compromise.

(c) Other firms have tried and mostly failed to copy TPS over 30+ years. GM's NUMMI joint venture with Toyota (1984-2010) gave GM direct exposure to TPS but GM could not replicate the system outside NUMMI because GM's culture, labor relations, and management approach were incompatible. Ford had some success under Alan Mulally but never fully matched Toyota. VW, Hyundai, and others have adopted elements of lean production but not the full system. The reason: TPS depends on causal ambiguity (hard to identify which elements produce the result), social complexity (culture and relationships), and path dependence (decades of accumulated learning). These are the classic inimitability factors in Barney's VRIO framework.

Some firms have gotten partway there: Honda has a distinct but comparable production system. Hyundai-Kia have substantially closed the quality gap while maintaining cost advantage. These successes show that Toyota's approach is not magic, but they also show how long it takes and how hard it is to replicate. Most firms attempting to copy TPS achieve moderate improvements but not Toyota's level of integration and consistency, because they cannot overcome their existing cultures and management systems.

Conclusion: Hybrid strategies are possible but extremely difficult. They require a specific operational capability that genuinely shifts the cost-quality frontier, and they cannot be easily copied. Most firms attempting hybrids without such a capability end up stuck in the middle - exactly what Porter warned against.

rubric.key: Correct assessment that Toyota is not stuck in the middle with reasoning
Specific TPS capabilities that break the cost-quality tradeoff
Discussion of imitation difficulty with specific competitor cases

questions.question: A regional airline is considering entering the US market with a focus differentiation strategy targeting business travelers. Analyze the strategic requirements, risks, and potential competitive responses.
questions.answer:
Focus Differentiation Strategy Analysis: New US Business-Travel Airline

Strategic Requirements:

1. Clear segmentation of target customer: business travelers are heterogeneous - road warriors needing frequent flights on specific routes, executives wanting premium cabins, small business owners valuing predictability, consultants needing flexible changes. The new airline must pick a specific business traveler sub-segment and design around their needs rather than 'business travelers in general.'

2. Differentiated product design: business travelers value time, productivity, and experience more than leisure travelers. Product features that differentiate could include: lie-flat seats on short-haul, WiFi and power on every seat, premium lounges at departure cities, priority boarding and baggage, quick ground service, flexible change policies, loyalty currency bonuses.

3. Route network matching business travel patterns: business travelers fly specific city pairs (NYC-LA, NYC-SF, Chicago-Atlanta) at specific times (weekday mornings and evenings). The airline should focus initially on 10-15 high-value business routes rather than trying to serve leisure markets.

4. Scheduling that serves business needs: multiple daily frequencies on high-value routes, morning and evening business departures, minimal ground connections.

5. Pricing premium justified by differentiation: fares 20-30% above mainstream airlines on the same routes, justified by the premium experience. Must resist the urge to compete on price during low-demand periods.

6. Operational capability to deliver reliably: business travelers are extremely delay-sensitive. On-time performance, aircraft reliability, and rebooking during disruptions must exceed industry averages.

Risks:

1. Limited market size: the premium business travel segment is smaller than leisure, limiting scale. Achieving break-even load factors on premium-priced seats is harder than on economy seats.

2. Direct competitive response from major airlines: Delta, United, and American have significant business traveler revenue and will aggressively defend it. Responses could include: matching premium features (premium cabins with lie-flat seats), targeted fare sales on affected routes, enhanced loyalty incentives, gate-proximity advantages from dominant airport positions.

3. Economic cycle vulnerability: business travel is highly cyclical, dropping sharply during recessions. A new entrant cannot weather a downturn the way established airlines can.

4. Pandemic/disruption risk: COVID-19 showed how quickly business travel can collapse. Virgin America, Eos, Silverjet, Virgin Atlantic Little Red - many premium-focused airlines have failed, often during travel disruptions.

5. Hub access and slots: major US business markets (JFK, LGA, DCA, SFO, ORD) have slot constraints that favor incumbent airlines. New entrants face access challenges.

6. Corporate contracts: business travel is substantially booked through corporate travel agreements that favor incumbents with global networks and loyalty programs. A new airline cannot offer a corporate traveler the global network needed for business trip flexibility.

7. Fleet commitments: a new airline must commit to aircraft orders with long lead times, creating financial risk if the business model does not work as expected.

Potential Competitive Responses from Incumbents:

1. Fare wars: major airlines can lower prices on affected routes to below new entrant's cost, absorbing short-term losses to drive out the competitor. This is the Southwest 'matching strategy' that has defeated many entrants.

2. Product matching: incumbents can upgrade their premium products on affected routes - better seats, more lounges, priority services - neutralizing the differentiator's advantage without abandoning the mass market.

3. Loyalty program incentives: elite status, bonus miles, and upgrades for business travelers who stay with incumbent - creating high switching costs.

4. Schedule matching: adding more frequent departures on the affected routes to match or exceed the new entrant's schedule, which undermines the new entrant's key differentiator.

5. Predatory scheduling: placing incumbent flights at nearly the same departure times to saturate capacity and reduce new entrant load factors.

6. Gate and slot restrictions: pressuring airports to limit the new entrant's access to key gates and slots.

Historical Examples:
- Virgin America (2007-2018): premium positioning with high-quality service and modern aircraft. Grew to meaningful share but never achieved sustainable profitability; acquired by Alaska Airlines and shut down. The business-focused premium model proved extremely hard to make economically viable given competitive responses.
- Eos Airlines (2005-2008): all-business-class transatlantic. Failed during 2008 recession.
- JetBlue Mint (2014-present): hybrid approach - JetBlue is a low-cost carrier that added a premium business cabin on select routes, creating a focused differentiation within a cost leader. Has been more successful than standalone premium entrants because the base cost structure is low.

Recommendation: Focus differentiation targeting business travelers on a limited number of high-value routes is theoretically attractive but historically very difficult to execute against incumbent competitive responses. Better approaches: (a) partner with or be acquired by an existing airline that provides the global network and operational scale; (b) focus on a specific subsegment where incumbents have weak offerings (e.g., small-city business travelers poorly served by hub-and-spoke); (c) pursue JetBlue Mint model - a low-cost carrier that adds premium differentiation on specific routes; or (d) focus on international premium segments where incumbents have less protective response capability.

rubric.key: Clear identification of strategic requirements including segment definition and product design
Realistic risk analysis addressing market size, competitive response, and cyclicality
Specific competitive response mechanisms with historical examples

questions.question: Kim and Mauborgne argue that 'blue ocean' strategies create uncontested market space while Porter emphasizes competing within defined industries. Are these frameworks compatible or contradictory? Use one example to illustrate your answer.
questions.answer:
The frameworks are substantially compatible, though they emphasize different strategic moves. Porter focuses on how to win within an industry by choosing a distinctive competitive position - cost leader, differentiator, or focused player - that creates sustainable advantage. Kim and Mauborgne argue that the highest-value strategic moves redefine market boundaries rather than fighting within existing ones, creating new value by combining features rivals don't offer and eliminating features rivals take for granted. Both agree that strategy requires distinctive positioning and that copying competitors is a losing game.

Key similarities:
1. Both reject 'stuck in the middle' strategies - pursuing features without coherent choices produces mediocre results.
2. Both emphasize that strategy requires saying no to certain activities (Porter: tradeoffs; Kim/Mauborgne: eliminate and reduce).
3. Both argue that sustainable advantage comes from system-level differentiation, not individual features.
4. Both use activity system thinking - Porter's activity maps and Kim/Mauborgne's strategy canvas are visually similar.

Key differences:
1. Porter treats industry boundaries as relatively fixed and emphasizes winning within them; Kim/Mauborgne treat boundaries as fluid and emphasize redrawing them.
2. Porter's frameworks (Five Forces, generic strategies) are analytic tools for understanding competitive dynamics; Kim/Mauborgne's frameworks (four actions, six paths) are creative tools for generating new strategy options.
3. Porter emphasizes capturing value within existing markets; Kim/Mauborgne emphasize creating new demand among non-customers.

Example: Cirque du Soleil

Porter's view would analyze Cirque du Soleil within the circus industry, noting that it differentiated itself through theatrical production values and targeted a premium adult audience rather than the traditional family market. Cirque's strategy would be classified as focused differentiation - a specific segment of the entertainment market served with a uniquely valuable product. From this view, Cirque is a sophisticated differentiator within the broader live entertainment industry.

Kim and Mauborgne's view argues that Cirque redefined the relevant industry entirely. They would say Cirque escaped the dying circus industry by creating a new category - theatrical circus - that drew from the entertainment values of theater and the physical performance of circus without being constrained by either industry's conventions. By eliminating animal acts (industry convention taken for granted), reducing fun/humor and thrill factors, raising the venue quality, and creating new features (theme, artistic music, refined environment), Cirque built something that neither theater nor circus incumbents offered.

Which is right? Both are partially correct. Cirque's value came from system-level differentiation (Porter's view) AND from boundary redefinition that unlocked new demand among non-customers - adults who would never attend a traditional circus (Kim/Mauborgne's view). The frameworks are complementary tools for understanding the same strategic move.

Synthesis: A comprehensive strategic analysis uses Porter's frameworks to understand current industry structure and identify where distinctive positioning is possible, then applies blue ocean thinking to identify opportunities to redefine boundaries and target non-customers. The best strategists use both lenses, not one to the exclusion of the other.

Practical implication: When analyzing a firm, ask two questions. First, what is its position within the current competitive landscape? (Porter's Five Forces, VRIO, generic strategies). Second, what boundary assumptions could be challenged to create new market space? (Kim/Mauborgne's four actions, six paths). If both yield insights, the firm has multiple strategic options to evaluate.

rubric.key: Clear identification of similarities and differences between frameworks
Specific example showing both frameworks applied to the same case
Synthesis arguing compatibility with practical implications

quizzes.topic: Disruptive Innovation, Platforms, and Competitive Dynamics

questions.question: Apply Christensen's disruptive innovation framework to Tesla vs. traditional automakers. (a) Is Tesla a disruptor in the Christensen sense? (b) If yes, what makes it disruptive? If no, why not? (c) How should Ford and GM respond?
questions.answer:
(a) Whether Tesla is a 'disruptor' in the strict Christensen sense is debated - including by Christensen himself before his death. The strict framework has specific requirements:
- Disruption starts at the low end (overserved customers) or in a new market (non-customers), not at the high end.
- The disruptive product is initially inferior on mainstream dimensions.
- It improves rapidly and eventually meets mainstream requirements, at which point it takes over.

Tesla does not fit perfectly. Tesla started at the HIGH end (Roadster at $109K in 2008, Model S at $77K in 2012) targeting premium customers, not low-end customers. Its early products were expensive luxury vehicles. This is closer to 'high-end strategic entry' than classic low-end disruption.

HOWEVER, Tesla has many disruption characteristics that align with Christensen's broader pattern:
- New technology (EV) with different performance dimensions (acceleration, software updates, over-the-air features) that traditional firms were late to pursue.
- Different business model (direct sales, no dealers; vertical integration on batteries and software).
- New value network (Supercharger network, software-first development) that legacy automakers struggle to replicate.
- Inferior on some traditional dimensions (charging time, range anxiety, service network) but improving rapidly.
- Threatens legacy automakers' core business (internal combustion vehicles) as adoption crosses the chasm to mainstream.

Christensen himself, in a 2015 HBR article with Raynor and McDonald, argued that Tesla does NOT meet the strict definition because it started at the high end. But the article acknowledged that the broader threat to incumbents is real and may play out as Tesla moves down-market (Model 3, eventually the $25K Tesla). If Model 3-and-below eventually dominates the mass market through a combination of cost curves, software advantages, and ecosystem lock-in, it will look like disruption in hindsight even if it didn't start that way.

Verdict: Tesla is a 'new-value-network entrant' more than a classic low-end disruptor. The strategic threat is similar regardless of terminology.

(b) What makes Tesla strategically threatening:

1. Vertical integration: Tesla owns battery manufacturing, motor design, software development, retail, and charging network. Legacy automakers depend on suppliers (batteries from LG/CATL, software from Harman/Microsoft, service from dealers). Tesla captures value legacy automakers don't.

2. Software-defined vehicles: Tesla vehicles improve over time through OTA updates. Legacy vehicles are static after purchase. This creates a value-over-time advantage that legacy automakers are still building capability to match.

3. Direct sales model: Tesla sells direct to customers, bypassing dealers. Legacy automakers are bound to dealer franchise laws in most US states and cannot easily match this.

4. Data advantage: Tesla has real-world data from billions of miles of driving collected through customer vehicles. This data trains autonomy algorithms and improves vehicle performance. Legacy automakers have less data.

5. Brand loyalty and cultural positioning: Tesla has become aspirational in a way that traditional auto brands have struggled to match for decades.

6. Manufacturing innovation: Giga presses, simplified body structures, rethink of manufacturing. Legacy automakers' existing factories cannot easily adopt these.

(c) How Ford and GM should respond:

1. Commit fully to EV transition rather than hedging. Ford's EV commitment ($30B+) and GM's ($35B+) are substantial but the execution has been uneven (e.g., Ford's F-150 Lightning ramp-up issues, GM's Ultium platform delays).

2. Separate EV operations from legacy organization. Ford tried this with 'Ford Model E' as a distinct unit in 2022, later partially reversed. GM has not fully separated. Christensen and O'Reilly & Tushman emphasize 'ambidextrous organization' - separate units to pursue disruptive opportunities without being strangled by the mainstream business. Legacy automakers struggle to give EV units the autonomy and capital discipline they need.

3. Build software capabilities aggressively. Hire software engineers, acquire software companies, change reward structures to attract and retain software talent. Legacy automakers are competing with tech companies for software talent and losing.

4. Rebuild dealer relationships or change the model. Direct-to-consumer is hard due to franchise laws, but some hybrid approaches (manufacturer-owned stores, online-first with dealer delivery) are emerging.

5. Invest in charging infrastructure. Ford and GM have partnered with Tesla on Supercharger access - a remarkable strategic move that acknowledges Tesla's network advantage and reduces it as a competitive differentiator.

6. Pursue manufacturing innovation. GM's Ultium platform and Ford's forthcoming platform aim at cost parity with Tesla. Execution matters enormously.

7. Rethink long-term business model. The future of automakers may be software and services revenue (subscriptions, over-the-air features, energy management) rather than one-time vehicle sales. Legacy automakers must reimagine their business model.

8. Consider strategic partnerships or M&A. Ford partnered with Rivian; GM acquired Cruise. Leveraging startups with different capabilities is faster than building from scratch.

The biggest risk: legacy automakers' rational response to their current shareholders (preserving ICE profitability, paying dividends, returning capital) is exactly what makes them vulnerable to disruption. The CEO who cannibalizes today's profitable ICE business to build tomorrow's EV business will face shareholder pressure. This is the innovator's dilemma in action.

rubric.key: Thoughtful analysis of whether Tesla meets Christensen's strict disruption definition
Multiple specific Tesla competitive advantages with evidence
Concrete, strategic response recommendations with feasibility analysis

questions.question: A new entrant wants to compete with Uber in a specific large US city. (a) Describe the chicken-and-egg problem they face. (b) List three strategies to overcome it. (c) Assess the likelihood of success against Uber's network effects.
questions.answer:
(a) Chicken-and-egg problem:

A two-sided platform like ride-sharing faces a bootstrap dilemma. Riders will not install and use the new app unless there are enough drivers to provide short wait times and reliable service. Drivers will not sign up with the new platform unless there are enough riders to provide steady earnings. But without drivers, there are no rides; without rides, there are no drivers. This is the defining challenge for any two-sided marketplace - you must solve 'no one will use it because no one uses it' before you have any users.

For a new ride-sharing entrant in a large city, the problem is particularly acute:
- Rider expectations: Uber has trained riders to expect wait times under 5 minutes in major cities. A new platform with 10-minute wait times looks broken, not new.
- Driver expectations: Drivers evaluate platforms on earnings per hour. If the new platform has low utilization (long gaps between rides), drivers earn less than on Uber/Lyft and will not stay.
- Both sides are multi-homing: drivers have Uber and Lyft apps already; riders have both apps. Neither has much incentive to add a third.
- Incumbents will respond: Uber and Lyft will subsidize both sides in affected areas to prevent the new entrant from gaining traction.

(b) Three strategies to overcome the chicken-and-egg problem:

1. Subsidize one side to critical mass, starting with supply (drivers).
Uber originally did this, paying early drivers guaranteed minimum earnings even when there were few rides. A new entrant could offer aggressive sign-on bonuses ($500-$2000), guaranteed minimum hourly rates for the first 30 days, and per-ride bonuses to attract drivers. Once driver supply reaches critical mass (enough to promise 5-minute wait times), the rider side can be marketed aggressively. The cost is substantial - possibly $10-20 million to seed a single large city with enough drivers for critical mass - but it solves the bootstrap problem directly.

Risk: Uber can match subsidies. If the new entrant's subsidies attract drivers, Uber will counter-subsidize to retain them. Drivers will multi-home, collecting bonuses from both platforms without committing. The new entrant must outlast incumbent subsidy responses, which requires deep pockets.

2. Geographic or niche focus to reach critical mass in a small market first.
Rather than launching citywide, focus on a specific neighborhood, airport, or use case where you can reach critical mass with fewer total drivers and riders. Airbnb started in specific neighborhoods before expanding; similar logic applies to ride-sharing. Examples: airport-only service (limited number of pickup locations, predictable demand), specific neighborhood with high demand density, specific use case (accessibility-focused rides, child-safe rides with background-checked drivers, EV-only rides for environmentally conscious riders).

The advantage: critical mass is achievable at lower cost because the geography or use case is smaller. Once the platform works in the niche, it can expand gradually. Risk: the niche may not be large enough to sustain a business, or incumbents may respond by adding niche-specific features (Uber added UberAssist, UberX Share, etc. to defend against niche entrants).

3. Marquee partnership or data advantage from adjacent business.
Leverage a pre-existing user base or business to bypass the bootstrap problem. Examples: DoorDash could leverage its delivery driver network to add ride-sharing. Waymo could leverage its autonomous vehicle capability to skip human drivers entirely. A grocery chain could offer ride-sharing to its store customers as a loyalty benefit. An airline could offer airport-to-home rides as part of its flight booking.

The advantage: you start with one side already populated (drivers from an existing business) or with bundled demand (riders brought from an adjacent service). The challenge: the adjacent business must share enough with ride-sharing to make the transition natural, and the adjacent business's economics must still work while supporting the new service.

Additional strategies:
4. Leverage autonomous vehicles to eliminate the driver supply problem. Waymo's approach - vehicles without drivers - completely sidesteps the driver bootstrap problem at the cost of requiring a massive capital investment and regulatory approval.

5. Regulatory advantage: a new entrant that is compliant with emerging gig-worker laws (employee classification, benefits) may attract regulatory support that undermines Uber's business model.

6. Differentiation that doesn't compete on network effects directly: focus on a fundamentally different experience (luxury, scheduled rides, specific demographics) where Uber's network effects are less relevant.

(c) Likelihood of success against Uber:

LOW but not zero. Uber has strong network effects in major cities, brand recognition, app ubiquity, and financial resources. Multiple well-funded entrants have failed: Sidecar (acquired and shut down), Via (pivoted away from consumer ride-sharing), Zimride (pivoted), Lyft (survived but has not dislodged Uber). The only successful US ride-sharing alternative to Uber is Lyft, which achieved 30-35% market share through sustained subsidization, differentiated brand positioning, and benefiting from Uber's periodic PR problems.

Conditions that could improve the odds:
- Regulatory disruption that forces Uber business model changes (driver reclassification as employees, city-level bans, or international expansion restrictions)
- Uber operational failures (app downtime, safety scandals, pricing backlash)
- Autonomous vehicle breakthrough that resets the competitive landscape
- Deep-pocketed strategic entry (Apple Car, Amazon ride-sharing, Chinese firms expanding abroad)

Without one of these disruptions, displacing Uber in a major US city is unlikely. The more realistic strategic move is niche entry - serving a segment Uber serves poorly, building critical mass in that niche, and expanding gradually. This is essentially the blue ocean approach: find uncontested market space rather than directly attacking the incumbent.

rubric.key: Correct explanation of chicken-and-egg problem with specific dynamics
Three substantive strategies with strengths and risks analyzed
Realistic likelihood assessment with historical evidence

questions.question: Explain three reasons why Kodak, with superior technology and a dominant market position, failed to successfully transition to digital photography despite inventing key digital imaging technologies.
questions.answer:
Kodak's failure to transition from film to digital photography is one of the most studied cases of incumbent disruption failure. It is particularly striking because Kodak invented the digital camera (Steven Sasson, 1975), owned thousands of digital imaging patents, and had the financial resources to dominate digital photography. Yet Kodak filed for bankruptcy in 2012. Three interconnected reasons explain this failure:

1. Profit protection over cannibalization.

Kodak's core business was selling film, which generated 70%+ gross margins. Each roll of film required additional Kodak products (development chemicals, photo paper, processing services) that reinforced the profit pool. Digital photography threatened this entire ecosystem: digital cameras don't use film, don't need development, and often don't result in printed photos. Every customer who switched from film to digital reduced Kodak's most profitable revenue stream.

Kodak's executives understood this. Internal analyses from the 1980s and 1990s projected that digital would eventually dominate, but always on a timeline that suggested 'we have more time.' Meanwhile, Kodak's resource allocation processes systematically funded film improvements over digital investment because film was more profitable per dollar of R&D.

This is the classic innovator's dilemma: rational capital allocation to the most profitable current business starves the investment needed to win the future business. Christensen specifically cited Kodak as an example.

Contrast: Fuji Film, Kodak's main competitor, made the opposite bet. Fuji aggressively diversified away from film into LCD films, cosmetics (using film chemistry expertise), medical imaging, and document management. Fuji survived and prospered; Kodak did not. Same industry, same technology transition, different strategic choices.

2. Capability mismatch and wrong business model for digital.

Kodak's core capabilities were in chemical engineering (film emulsions), coating technology, and mass production of film. None of these transferred to digital photography, which required capabilities in digital signal processing, integrated circuit design, software development, and consumer electronics manufacturing.

Kodak had little expertise in these areas. Worse, the capabilities are located in different industry ecosystems: Silicon Valley for chips and software, Asia for consumer electronics manufacturing. Kodak was based in Rochester, NY, far from either ecosystem. Recruiting the right talent, building the right relationships, and executing at the speed of consumer electronics markets required organizational transformations Kodak did not achieve.

Even when Kodak did enter digital cameras (the EasyShare line, 2001-2012), the business model was wrong. Kodak tried to apply its 'razor and blades' film-era model to digital: sell cameras cheap and make money on photo printing. But digital photography produced printed images at far lower rates than film (most digital photos never leave the camera or phone), and photo paper itself commoditized quickly. The business model assumption failed.

3. Organizational inertia and cultural resistance.

Kodak's organization was built around film: its engineers had spent careers perfecting film emulsions, its factories produced film, its distributors sold film, its retail partners developed film, and its marketing celebrated film-based photography. Asking this organization to pivot to digital required changing every one of these elements simultaneously - and the people who had built Kodak's film business were emotionally and professionally invested in it.

Internal digital advocates reported being ignored, marginalized, or overruled by film-focused executives. R&D dollars went to marginal improvements in film quality rather than digital sensor development. Manufacturing investments went to new film lines rather than digital imaging capability. Marketing continued to emphasize 'Kodak Moments' built around film photography.

The organizational structure reflected film: divisions for consumer film, professional film, graphic arts film, motion picture film. Digital did not fit naturally into any division and was therefore starved of attention and resources.

By 2000, Kodak had actually become a digital camera leader by market share in the US - but made no money on digital, funded the digital effort from declining film profits, and could not extract digital value. By 2008, smartphones obviated standalone cameras entirely, and Kodak had neither film revenue to fund transition nor digital platform to compete.

Conclusion: Kodak had the technology. It had the capital. It had the market position. It failed because its profit protection instincts, capability mismatches, and organizational inertia made rational adaptation impossible. This is a textbook case of how incumbent success creates incumbent vulnerability.

Lessons for contemporary firms:
- Protecting today's profits is rational in the short run and catastrophic in the long run when disruption arrives.
- Building new capabilities requires more than hiring a few experts - it requires reshaping the entire organization.
- Organizational inertia is the hardest problem. Transformation programs that don't address culture, rewards, and identity typically fail.
- Separating the disruptive unit from the core business (as Netflix did when launching streaming) can preserve the new business from the old organization's killing instincts.

rubric.key: Three substantive reasons with specific mechanisms and evidence
Comparison to successful transitions (Fuji, Netflix) for contrast
Connection to broader strategic frameworks (innovator's dilemma, capability mismatch)

questions.question: A diversified conglomerate earning below cost of capital is considering three options: (a) divest non-core businesses and refocus, (b) acquire a fast-growing tech company to boost growth, (c) improve operational performance across all existing businesses. Evaluate each option using corporate strategy frameworks and recommend an approach.
questions.answer:
Corporate Strategy Options Evaluation:

Option A: Divest non-core businesses and refocus

Analytical basis: Porter's three tests for diversification (attractiveness, cost of entry, better-off) often reveal that existing business portfolios fail one or more tests. If the conglomerate is earning below cost of capital, several businesses are likely destroying value rather than creating it. Divestiture frees capital, management attention, and organizational focus for businesses where the corporation has genuine advantage.

Evidence for this approach: Spin-offs and divestitures have historically produced superior shareholder returns. Research by Cusatis, Miles & Woolridge (1993, 1995) and subsequent studies show that parent companies and spun-off subsidiaries both outperform the market after separation. Recent examples: GE's three-way split into aerospace, healthcare, and energy (announced 2021, completed 2024) has produced significant value creation for each piece. Abbott Laboratories spun off pharmaceutical division as AbbVie in 2013; both companies have outperformed the broader market. DuPont spun off performance materials into Chemours, then split the remaining company into Corteva and DuPont (new). In each case, separation unlocked value that conglomerate structure suppressed.

Consequences: Divestitures are typically followed by improved operational performance in divested units (freedom from conglomerate overhead, industry-specific capital discipline, better aligned incentives) and improved focus in the parent (attention to genuinely advantaged businesses, elimination of 'conglomerate discount' valuation). The pain is in the transition: layoffs, executive reshuffling, stranded costs.

Risks: Divestitures can destroy synergies that were real (shared R&D, customer relationships, procurement scale). Must carefully identify which businesses actually share capabilities vs. which are held together by historical accident.

Option B: Acquire a fast-growing tech company

Analytical basis: Apply Porter's three tests rigorously.

Attractiveness test: Is the target industry structurally attractive? Fast-growing tech industries often look attractive but can be hypercompetitive (low barriers to entry, winner-take-all platform dynamics, intense talent competition). Many 'high growth' industries have very poor profitability - food delivery, ride-sharing, cloud infrastructure (outside the big three) are fast-growing and mostly unprofitable.

Cost of entry test: Fast-growing tech companies are almost always expensive. Acquisition premiums run 30-50% above market, and market valuations of tech are typically high relative to current earnings. The cost of entry often consumes the future value the target can deliver. Example: Microsoft's $26.2B purchase of LinkedIn in 2016 was criticized as overpaying; the acquisition has been reasonably successful but needed years to justify the price.

Better-off test: Will the target perform better as part of the conglomerate, or will the conglomerate perform better because of the target? This is the key question. Most diversifying tech acquisitions fail this test. The conglomerate typically has nothing to offer the tech target (no shared customers, no shared technology, no shared culture), and the target offers nothing to the conglomerate except growth optics. Both deteriorate after the acquisition.

Evidence: Most large diversifying tech acquisitions have failed. AT&T/NCR (1991, $7.4B write-off), AOL/Time Warner (2000, history's largest value destruction), HP/Autonomy (2011, $8.8B write-down), Microsoft/Nokia (2014, $7.6B write-off). Every few years, an old-economy firm buys a tech firm and destroys value.

Successful cases exist but are rare: Disney acquiring Pixar (2006) worked because Disney had genuine animation capability and Pixar filled a technological gap. Facebook acquiring Instagram (2012) worked because Facebook's platform capabilities accelerated Instagram's growth. These are exceptions, not the rule.

Recommendation on Option B: Do NOT pursue acquisitions as a way to boost growth unless Porter's three tests are rigorously passed. 'We need growth' is not a valid reason to acquire.

Option C: Improve operational performance across all existing businesses

Analytical basis: If the conglomerate earns below cost of capital, one possibility is that its businesses are structurally sound but operationally underperforming. Consulting firms make careers on this diagnosis - 'you have great businesses, just need better execution.'

Evidence: Operational improvements can generate meaningful value. Lean operations, technology modernization, procurement consolidation, HR improvements all add up. Jack Welch's GE pursued this relentlessly for 20 years and drove market cap from $13B to $500B, though much of GE's growth was driven by GE Capital's financial services rather than operational improvements.

However: Operational improvements alone cannot transform a conglomerate earning below cost of capital into a value creator. The underlying portfolio problem remains. You can run bad businesses well, but they are still bad businesses. Operational excellence is necessary but not sufficient.

Recommendation:

The best answer is A + some of C, with explicit rejection of B.

Phase 1 (immediate): Conduct rigorous portfolio analysis using Porter's three tests. Identify businesses where the corporation adds value versus those where it does not. Divest businesses that fail the better-off test (where the business would be more valuable on its own). Target 30-50% divestiture of current revenue.

Phase 2 (12-24 months): Use proceeds from divestitures to (a) return capital to shareholders through dividends or buybacks, (b) invest in operational improvements in retained businesses, (c) possibly tuck-in acquisitions that strengthen core businesses (not diversifying acquisitions).

Phase 3 (24-36 months): Drive operational excellence in the refocused portfolio. With fewer businesses to manage, leadership attention and capital can be focused on high-value improvements.

Avoid: Diversifying into new industries through acquisitions. The conglomerate's underperformance is the evidence that its existing diversification is not working; more diversification will not fix it.

Historical precedent: This is essentially the strategy that Tyco International pursued after the Kozlowski scandal (2002-2012), the strategy that Honeywell pursued under David Cote, and the strategy that GE is pursuing now. In each case, refocusing plus operational improvement has created significant shareholder value.

rubric.key: Correct application of Porter's three tests to evaluate each option
Evidence-based reasoning including historical examples
Clear recommendation with sequenced implementation plan

questions.question: Explain why the McKinsey 7S framework argues that strategy cannot be implemented by changing strategy alone. Use the failed Ron Johnson transformation at JC Penney to illustrate.
questions.answer:
The McKinsey 7S framework's core claim is that organizations are systems of interdependent elements - Strategy, Structure, Systems, Style (leadership), Staff (people), Skills (capabilities), and Shared Values (culture) - and that changing one element without changing the others fails because the unchanged elements drag the organization back toward its original state. Strategy changes must be accompanied by changes in structure, systems, incentives, people, skills, and culture, or execution will fail regardless of how brilliant the strategy is.

The framework's insight is almost counterintuitive: the hard part of strategy is not formulation but the simultaneous transformation of the six other elements that support it. A new strategy drops into an existing organization like a fresh organ into an incompatible body - without the full transplant of supporting systems, it will be rejected.

The Ron Johnson transformation at JC Penney (2011-2013) is one of the clearest cautionary tales in recent corporate history. Johnson, former Apple Retail executive who had built the Apple Store into the world's highest-revenue-per-square-foot retailer, was hired in November 2011 to transform JC Penney from a struggling, promotional, middle-market department store into a modern experiential retailer.

Johnson's strategy was bold and theoretically coherent:
- Eliminate the heavy promotional pricing model (coupons, sales) in favor of 'everyday low prices' and 'monthly fair and square pricing.'
- Create store-within-store boutiques featuring premium brands and designer partnerships (Martha Stewart, Michael Graves, Sephora).
- Build a modern, experiential store environment with a 'town square' feel.
- Target a younger, more affluent customer base.
- Launch a new logo, advertising, and marketing campaign.

In isolation, these ideas were defensible. Some had worked elsewhere (Target's cheap-chic positioning, Sephora's shop-in-shops). But Johnson changed Strategy and Marketing without changing the other 7S elements that needed to change, and the execution collapsed.

Structure: JC Penney's organization was built around promotional retail - merchandising teams ran sale cycles, regional managers optimized coupon redemption, buyers sourced value-oriented goods. Johnson kept this structure largely intact while asking it to execute a fundamentally different strategy. The existing structure could not support boutique operations, premium brand relationships, or experiential retail.

Systems: JC Penney's IT systems, supply chain, pricing tools, and inventory management were built for promotional retail. Moving to everyday low pricing required different analytics (customer lifetime value vs. promotion response modeling), different supply chain cadence, and different merchandise planning. The systems were not changed fast enough to support the new strategy.

Style: Johnson's leadership style was Silicon Valley product visionary, emphasizing bold bets and fast decisions. The existing JC Penney culture was consensus-driven and cautious. Johnson did not adapt his style to the organization he inherited and reportedly dismissed input from veteran executives who had seen similar ideas fail. The style mismatch created rapid executive turnover.

Staff: Johnson replaced many senior executives with Silicon Valley talent (many from Apple) who had no experience in traditional retail. The new executives lacked JC Penney-specific knowledge, relationships with suppliers, and understanding of the core customer base. Meanwhile, veteran retail executives left.

Skills: JC Penney's core retail skills (promotional retail, middle-market merchandising, value positioning) were the wrong skills for the new strategy. But JC Penney did not have experiential retail skills, boutique operation skills, or premium brand relationship management skills. The transformation assumed capabilities that did not exist.

Shared Values: JC Penney's culture valued serving middle-class American shoppers with value-oriented products and good service. The new strategy asked this culture to serve younger, more affluent customers with premium brands. This cultural shift did not happen quickly, and many employees felt alienated from the new direction.

The results of this misalignment were catastrophic:
- Core customers, alienated by the elimination of promotions and coupons (which they loved and had been trained to expect), stopped shopping at JC Penney.
- The target younger, affluent customers did not arrive in sufficient numbers to replace them - they already shopped at Target, Nordstrom Rack, and specialty stores.
- Sales collapsed 25% in 2012 alone, the worst in JC Penney's history.
- Same-store sales dropped 31.7% in Q1 2013.
- Johnson was fired in April 2013, 17 months after taking the CEO role.
- JC Penney's market cap fell from $8B to $2B.
- The company eventually filed for bankruptcy in 2020 (though many factors contributed).

In contrast, compare this to Satya Nadella's successful transformation of Microsoft starting 2014.
