Nadella changed Strategy (cloud-first, cross-platform) but also changed Structure (reorganized around cloud, eliminated stack-ranking), Systems (new metrics around cloud growth, new compensation tied to Azure), Style (collaborative leadership replacing Ballmer's combative style), Staff (hired cloud executives, changed senior leadership), Skills (upskilled for cloud and AI), and Shared Values (growth mindset replacing know-it-all culture). All seven elements changed together. Microsoft's market cap grew from $300B to $3T+.

The lesson is unambiguous: strategic transformation is not a matter of announcing a new strategy. It requires changing every reinforcing element simultaneously. Johnson changed strategy and hoped the rest would follow. Nadella changed strategy and simultaneously rebuilt the organization. The outcomes are the difference between catastrophic failure and the most successful turnaround in corporate history.

For any strategic leader: before announcing a new strategy, diagnose whether your existing structure, systems, staff, skills, style, and shared values can support it. If not, plan explicitly for how each element will change. Otherwise, the new strategy will die in implementation regardless of its analytical merits.

rubric.key: Clear explanation of 7S interdependence and why alignment is required
Detailed JC Penney case analysis with specific 7S misalignments identified
Contrast with successful case (Microsoft) showing aligned transformation

questions.question: A CEO is deciding whether to enter China with a wholly owned subsidiary, a joint venture with a Chinese partner, or through licensing to a Chinese company. Identify three factors that should drive this decision and give one example of a firm that chose each approach.
questions.answer:
The choice between wholly-owned subsidiary (WOS), joint venture (JV), and licensing for China entry is one of the most consequential international business decisions. Each approach involves different tradeoffs between control, risk, learning, and capability access. Three factors should drive the choice:

1. Strategic importance of protecting proprietary technology or brand

If the firm's competitive advantage depends on proprietary technology, brand, or operational know-how that could be stolen or diluted through a local partner, WOS is strongly preferred despite higher cost. Joint ventures require sharing know-how with partners who may later become competitors, and Chinese partners have a documented history of using JV access to build independent capabilities.

Licensing is even worse for advantage protection - it explicitly transfers technology to licensees who may not honor IP terms.

Example - WOS: Tesla's Shanghai Gigafactory (opened 2019). Tesla negotiated unprecedented terms with the Chinese government: 100% ownership of the factory, no forced JV partner, and autonomy over technology and operations. This was possible because China wanted Tesla's EV technology and manufacturing capability to jumpstart its own EV industry. Tesla's bet paid off - the Shanghai factory became Tesla's most profitable and productive facility, producing over 700,000 vehicles per year. Tesla kept its manufacturing secrets while gaining massive scale in the world's largest EV market.

Example - JV: Nearly all traditional automakers (GM, VW, Toyota, BMW) entered China through 50-50 joint ventures because Chinese regulations required it until 2018-2022 for passenger vehicles. VW's Shanghai Automotive Industry Corporation (SAIC) joint venture from 1985 became China's largest car producer. The tradeoff: VW gained access to the Chinese market and shared profits with SAIC, but also shared technology and operational know-how. SAIC has since developed its own auto brands (Roewe, MG) that compete with VW globally. The JV built a Chinese competitor out of VW's own technology - a classic example of the long-term risk of JVs.

2. Market knowledge and regulatory navigation requirements

If the host country is complex to navigate (regulatory, cultural, logistical), a local partner's contributions become more valuable. China's regulatory environment, local consumer preferences, distribution systems, government relationships, and HR practices require deep local knowledge. A JV partner can provide this knowledge and speed up market entry. WOS requires the firm to build this knowledge from scratch, which is slow and risky.

For firms without China experience, JV may be the right starting point even if WOS would be preferred long-term.

Example - JV: Yum Brands (KFC, Pizza Hut) entered China primarily through joint ventures and franchisees with strong local partners who handled real estate, government relations, and local adaptation. KFC became one of the largest restaurant chains in China with over 9,000 outlets, largely because of partnership-driven localization (rice bowls, congee, green tea). Yum eventually spun off Yum China as a publicly traded entity in 2016, recognizing that the China business was different enough to warrant a separate structure.

Example - Licensing: McDonald's in China (2017 restructuring): sold 80% of its China operations to a Chinese consortium (CITIC Capital and Carlyle Group). This was effectively a licensing / franchise arrangement where McDonald's retained the brand, operational standards, and royalty stream but gave operational control to local partners. The rationale: local partners could navigate the Chinese market faster than McDonald's directly-managed approach.

3. Capital and risk tolerance

WOS requires the full capital investment and absorbs all downside risk. JV shares capital and risk with the partner. Licensing requires minimal capital and offers limited upside but minimal downside.

For firms entering China as a strategic priority, WOS or JV makes sense because the return potential justifies the capital. For firms where China is a secondary market or experimental, licensing offers a way to gain presence without commitment.

Example - Licensing: Apparel brands often license their trademarks to Chinese manufacturers and retailers. Disney has used extensive licensing in China for consumer products, allowing Chinese firms to produce Mickey Mouse merchandise, Marvel toys, etc. under trademark agreements. Disney captures royalty revenue without operational risk. However, Disney's theme parks (Shanghai Disney) are joint ventures with Shanghai Shendi Group to combine Disney's operating capability with local real estate, government relations, and capital.

Additional factors to consider:

4. Host government requirements: China has historically required JVs in many sectors (automotive, financial services, certain tech sectors). These requirements have been loosening since 2018, but political risk remains. Recent examples: Tesla was granted WOS in 2019, BMW was granted majority stake in its JV in 2022, financial services ownership rules liberalized in 2018-2020.

5. Exit considerations: JVs are very hard to exit cleanly. Partners often disagree on valuation, governance, and strategic direction. Anbang Insurance, HNA, Dalian Wanda all experienced contentious exits from foreign partnerships. WOS exits are simpler. Licensing can typically be wound down at contract expiration.

6. Trust in partners: The quality and track record of potential JV partners matters. Some Chinese companies have reputations as reliable partners (Midea, Haier, Alibaba); others have histories of opportunistic behavior. Partner selection is as important as the entry mode decision.

Recommendation framework: Default to WOS if advantage protection is critical AND regulatory feasible AND capital is available. Default to JV if local knowledge is critical OR required by regulation OR risk reduction is valued. Default to licensing if China is secondary priority OR operational commitment is not justified. Review the decision periodically as markets and regulations evolve.

rubric.key: Three substantive factors clearly explained
Specific examples for each entry mode with context
Synthesis showing tradeoffs and decision framework

questions.question: Analyze Satya Nadella's transformation of Microsoft (2014-present) using Rumelt's kernel of good strategy (diagnosis, guiding policy, coherent actions).
questions.answer:
Rumelt's kernel of good strategy has three components: (1) a diagnosis that identifies the real challenge, (2) a guiding policy that describes the approach to dealing with the challenge, and (3) coherent actions that implement the guiding policy. Satya Nadella's transformation of Microsoft is one of the clearest examples of this structure in modern corporate history.

DIAGNOSIS: When Nadella became CEO in February 2014, Microsoft faced a specific, clearly identifiable challenge. The firm had become a 'Windows-first' company in a world where Windows was no longer the universal computing platform. Mobile devices (iOS, Android) had disrupted the PC-centric computing model. Cloud computing was shifting value from on-premises software to cloud services. Google, Apple, Amazon, and Facebook had captured the consumer and cloud markets while Microsoft remained focused on defending Windows and Office on PCs. Internal culture had become cynical under Ballmer's stack-ranking system and combative management style. Stock price had been flat for over a decade while competitors' had grown 10x. Developers were abandoning the Microsoft platform for iOS, Android, and web technologies.

The core diagnostic insight: Microsoft's strength was not Windows. Microsoft's strength was enterprise relationships, productivity software, and developer tools. Windows had been the delivery mechanism for those strengths, but Windows was no longer the only delivery mechanism. Insisting that customers use Microsoft products ONLY through Windows was losing customers to competitors who offered cloud and cross-platform alternatives. The challenge was: how do we reorient Microsoft around its actual strengths (enterprise productivity, cloud services, developer tools) and stop being held hostage to declining Windows relevance?

This diagnosis was clear and correct. Previous Microsoft leadership had treated the mobile and cloud transitions as problems to be fought (through Windows Phone, Nokia acquisition, SaaS competition). Nadella reframed them as facts to be accepted and built around.

GUIDING POLICY: Nadella's guiding policy was 'Cloud First, Mobile First' (announced March 2014) and later 'Intelligent Cloud, Intelligent Edge.' The policy committed Microsoft to three major strategic directions:

1. Cloud First: Azure would be the center of Microsoft's strategy, competing directly with AWS. Investment, talent, and executive attention would flow to cloud services rather than Windows.

2. Mobile First (but not 'Microsoft Mobile'): Microsoft would bring its productivity tools to iOS and Android rather than forcing users onto Windows devices. This was a massive cultural shift - Microsoft would explicitly support competitor platforms.

3. Platform over Lock-In: Microsoft would become a platform company that developers and customers want to use, not a platform they are forced to use. Open source, cross-platform, and developer-friendly became strategic priorities.

A guiding policy is not a plan; it is an approach that shapes many subsequent decisions. Nadella's 'Cloud First, Mobile First' answered hundreds of specific strategic questions: Should we invest in Windows Phone? No. Should we port Office to iOS? Yes. Should Azure support Linux workloads? Yes. Should we open-source .NET? Yes. Should we acquire LinkedIn? Yes (aligned with the productivity/cloud strategy). Should we acquire GitHub? Yes (aligned with developer platform strategy). Should we make Minecraft cross-platform? Yes.

COHERENT ACTIONS: Nadella's guiding policy was executed through coordinated actions across the organization:

1. Product actions:
- Office 365 prioritized over Office on Windows - subscription cloud model became default.
- Office on iOS and Android released (2014) for free, reversing years of artificial restriction.
- Azure accelerated with new services, regions, and enterprise integration.
- Windows Phone gradually abandoned (Nokia acquisition written down in 2015 for $7.6B).
- .NET open-sourced and brought to Linux (2014).
- GitHub acquired (2018) for $7.5B, making Microsoft the largest host of developer code.
- LinkedIn acquired (2016) for $26.2B, adding to productivity suite and enterprise services.
- Minecraft acquired (2014) for $2.5B, brought to all platforms.
- Activision Blizzard acquired (2023) for $69B, expanding gaming platform.

2. Organizational actions:
- Reorganization to focus on cloud, productivity, and computing rather than Windows/Office silos.
- Eliminated stack-ranking performance management (Ballmer era).
- Culture transformation emphasizing 'growth mindset' (from Carol Dweck's research), replacing 'know-it-all' culture with 'learn-it-all' culture.
- Hired Scott Guthrie to lead Azure, Kevin Scott as CTO, Amy Hood as CFO - all aligned with the new direction.

3. Financial actions:
- Capital allocation shifted to cloud infrastructure and acquisitions that supported the guiding policy.
- Dividend and buyback continued for shareholders but not at the expense of strategic investment.
- R&D spend increased and shifted toward cloud, AI, and productivity.

4. Ecosystem actions:
- Partnership with Amazon (Alexa/Cortana integration), Apple (Office on iPad Pro), Salesforce (co-developments), even previous rivals - ending the Windows-first tribalism.
- OpenAI partnership and investment starting 2019, which positioned Microsoft as the most credible enterprise AI company when ChatGPT arrived in 2022.

Results (as of 2026):
- Market cap: grew from $300B in February 2014 to over $3 trillion in 2024, making Microsoft occasionally the most valuable company in the world.
- Azure: became the #2 cloud platform globally with over $100B annualized revenue, closing the gap on AWS.
- Office 365: over 400M paid subscribers worldwide.
- Developer platform: GitHub, VS Code, .NET have become the industry standard tools, reversing a decade of developer mindshare loss.
- AI leadership: OpenAI partnership positioned Microsoft as the leading AI platform for enterprise.
- Cultural transformation: Microsoft is now consistently rated among the best places to work in tech.

Why this analysis fits Rumelt's framework:

Rumelt argues that most organizations have 'bad strategy' because they skip the diagnosis step or cannot commit to a guiding policy that requires hard choices. Microsoft's previous leadership had diagnosed the mobile and cloud transitions but could not commit to the guiding policy (abandoning Windows-first) because it required cannibalizing profitable businesses. Nadella's strategic clarity came from a diagnosis he was willing to act on, a guiding policy that required real tradeoffs (accepting that Windows Phone would fail, accepting that Office would be on competitor platforms), and coherent actions that all pointed in the same direction.

This is the hallmark of good strategy: diagnosis, policy, and action all fit together. The actions were not random initiatives; they all implemented the same guiding policy. Had any of them been contradictory (e.g., launching a new Windows Phone while also committing to cross-platform), the transformation would have failed. Commitment to coherent direction is what distinguishes good strategy from bad.

Lesson for strategic leaders: before executing actions, make sure you have a clear diagnosis and committed guiding policy. Actions without diagnosis produce random initiatives. Diagnosis without guiding policy produces analysis paralysis. Guiding policy without coherent action produces broken execution. All three are needed.

rubric.key: Clear diagnosis phase identifying Microsoft's real challenge
Articulation of Nadella's guiding policy and its strategic commitments
Specific coherent actions that implemented the policy with results

questions.question: A company's leadership team is preparing a strategic decision that will commit $2B and several years of attention. How could they use a pre-mortem to improve the decision? What biases is the pre-mortem intended to counter?
questions.answer:
A pre-mortem (developed by psychologist Gary Klein) is a decision-making technique where a team imagines that a planned decision has failed - before making the decision - and works backward to identify why it failed. The technique counteracts several well-documented cognitive biases that plague strategic decision-making.

How to run a pre-mortem on a $2B strategic decision:

Step 1: Convene the decision-making team after the strategic analysis is complete but before the commitment is made. This should be the same group that will actually decide - their psychological buy-in to the process matters.

Step 2: Present the premise. The facilitator says: 'Assume we have made this decision and committed the $2B. It's now three years later. The initiative has failed catastrophically. The press is calling it a disaster. Shareholders are furious. People are losing their jobs. Now: work backward and tell me what went wrong.'

Step 3: Each team member writes down (individually, not in discussion) all the reasons the failure might have happened. Individual writing prevents the most senior person's views from anchoring the discussion and enables quiet voices to contribute.

Step 4: Collect all the failure modes and group them. Typical categories:
- External factors that changed (market, technology, regulation, geopolitics).
- Internal execution failures (talent, capability, timing, coordination).
- Competitive responses (rivals acted in ways not anticipated).
- Customer response (customers didn't adopt, switched, or rejected).
- Financial assumptions that proved wrong (cost overruns, revenue shortfalls).
- Strategic misalignment (initiative conflicted with other priorities).
- Leadership or governance failures (key people left, strategy drifted, board lost patience).

Step 5: Rank the failure modes by likelihood and severity. Identify the top 5-10 risks that deserve attention.

Step 6: For each top risk, develop specific mitigations:
- Avoid the risk (redesign the initiative to eliminate the failure mode).
- Reduce the risk (change how the work will be done to make failure less likely).
- Transfer the risk (insurance, partnerships, contract protections).
- Monitor the risk (establish early warning indicators and decision points).
- Accept the risk (if mitigation cost exceeds risk value, decide consciously to accept).

Step 7: Update the decision with explicit risk mitigations incorporated. If the residual risk is too high, the decision may change from 'go' to 'go with different scope' or 'don't go.'

Step 8: Document the exercise so that if problems emerge during execution, the team can check against the pre-mortem analysis and respond faster.

Biases the pre-mortem counters:

1. Optimism bias / planning fallacy: Teams systematically overestimate the likelihood of success and underestimate time, cost, and risk of planned projects. Kahneman and Tversky documented this in 'planning fallacy' research - projects consistently run over budget and schedule because planners imagine best-case execution, not realistic execution. The pre-mortem forces teams to take failure seriously rather than dismissing it.

2. Confirmation bias: Teams seek information confirming their preferred direction and ignore information suggesting problems. Once a strategic direction is chosen, confirming evidence gets attention and disconfirming evidence gets dismissed. The pre-mortem explicitly invites disconfirming thinking.

3. Groupthink: In high-status groups, senior leaders' views anchor discussion and junior members hesitate to raise concerns. Over time, groupthink produces homogeneous views and systematic blind spots. The pre-mortem's individual writing step bypasses groupthink by collecting everyone's concerns before anchoring.

4. Sunk cost fallacy (prospective version): Once a team has invested significant analysis effort in a decision, they become committed to the conclusion because revising it would 'waste' the analysis. The pre-mortem rescues decisions from premature commitment.

5. Affect heuristic: Decisions are influenced by emotional reactions (excitement about new opportunities, fear of being left behind, desire to be bold) rather than evidence. The pre-mortem counters by directing attention to specific failure mechanisms.

6. Hindsight bias neutralization: Hindsight bias (the 'I knew it all along' feeling after failure) makes post-mortems less useful for learning. The pre-mortem extracts the insight that would come from hindsight - 'why did this fail?' - before the hindsight bias can distort it.

7. Overconfidence: Individual overconfidence in one's own judgment is pervasive. Teams dominated by overconfident leaders make worse decisions. The pre-mortem's structure gives space for more cautious perspectives that overconfident individuals might otherwise suppress.

8. Availability heuristic: Teams give weight to recent, vivid, or easily imagined outcomes and underweight outcomes that are statistically important but harder to imagine. Pre-mortem broadens the set of imagined futures to include failure cases, counteracting this bias.

Why pre-mortem works better than just 'asking for concerns':

Asking 'what could go wrong?' in a meeting where the CEO has stated a preference rarely produces candid responses. People are reluctant to criticize the boss's idea, reluctant to be the 'negative person,' and reluctant to look like they don't understand the opportunity. The pre-mortem's premise ('assume it failed') gives everyone permission and cover to identify failure modes without appearing to oppose leadership.

Research on pre-mortem effectiveness (Klein 2007, Mitchell et al. 1989) has shown that groups given a 'prospective hindsight' premise generate 30% more reasons for potential outcomes than groups simply asked to predict the future. The cognitive shift from 'forecast' to 'explain the already-known result' accesses different mental models and surfaces concerns that wouldn't otherwise emerge.

Limitations: Pre-mortems require psychological safety (people must be able to voice concerns without retribution), cognitive effort, and time. They work less well for small decisions where the effort outweighs the benefit. They can be gamed if the leader doesn't take the outputs seriously. And they don't replace good analysis - they complement it.

For the $2B strategic decision in the question, a pre-mortem would likely save 10-30% of the investment by identifying risks that would otherwise emerge during execution. The couple of hours invested in the pre-mortem is trivial compared to the $2B at stake. Pre-mortem should be standard practice for any major strategic commitment.

rubric.key: Clear pre-mortem process with specific steps
Multiple cognitive biases identified with specific mechanisms
Practical limitations and context for when pre-mortem is most useful

questions.question: Write a 35-word strategy statement (in the Collis & Rukstad format) for a business of your choice, including objective, scope, and advantage. Then explain each component.
questions.answer:
Collis and Rukstad's 2008 HBR article 'Can You Say What Your Strategy Is?' argues that most executives cannot articulate their firm's strategy in a concise, coherent way. They propose a 35-word strategy statement format with three components: Objective (what you want to achieve), Scope (the domain in which you will operate), and Advantage (the means by which you will win).

Example Strategy Statement: Stripe

'To grow the GDP of the internet by providing software infrastructure for global online commerce, serving technically sophisticated businesses worldwide, through developer-first APIs, financial infrastructure scale, and trust built through reliable global payment processing.'

(34 words)

Component Analysis:

Objective: 'To grow the GDP of the internet'

Stripe's stated mission is to 'increase the GDP of the internet' - a specific, measurable, aspirational goal that goes beyond generic profit maximization. The objective tells employees, customers, and investors why Stripe exists and what they are trying to achieve. It is bold enough to motivate effort but concrete enough to guide decisions.

A weak objective would be: 'to be the best payment company.' Weak because 'best' is undefined and 'payment company' is generic. Stripe's objective is specific - it describes the desired end state (a larger internet economy) that all Stripe activities should contribute to.

Scope: 'providing software infrastructure for global online commerce, serving technically sophisticated businesses worldwide'

Scope answers three questions: What product? What customers? What geography?

Product: software infrastructure for online commerce. Not physical payment terminals (vs. Square), not retail store POS systems (vs. Toast), not just payment processing (vs. PayPal). Stripe sells infrastructure - APIs, SDKs, fraud tools, billing systems, tax compliance, and related financial services - to power online commerce. This scope decision excludes in-person retail, excludes simple hobbyist payment acceptance, and excludes becoming a consumer-facing financial brand.

Customers: technically sophisticated businesses. Stripe's primary customer is a software developer or technical business that wants to integrate payments into their own product. Stripe does not target mom-and-pop retailers who want a simple way to accept cards (Square's market). Stripe does not target casual users who want peer-to-peer money transfers (Venmo/Cash App's market). The scope is specific and disciplined.

Geography: worldwide. Stripe is expanding globally, supporting payments in most major countries. This is ambitious scope but appropriate for a software infrastructure company where scale economies reward broad coverage.

The scope decision forces tradeoffs: Stripe is not trying to be all things to all payment users. It is deliberately focused on a specific segment (technical businesses doing online commerce) where its capabilities create disproportionate value. Other firms serve other segments.

Advantage: 'through developer-first APIs, financial infrastructure scale, and trust built through reliable global payment processing'

Advantage answers: why will we win? What makes us different and hard to imitate?

Developer-first APIs: Stripe's first competitive advantage is the quality of its developer experience. Stripe APIs are well-documented, consistent, easy to integrate, and reliable. For a technical business making an integration decision, Stripe's developer experience is dramatically better than alternatives. This advantage comes from an organizational focus on developer needs and years of refinement, not a single feature.

Financial infrastructure scale: Stripe has become one of the largest online payment processors globally, handling over $1 trillion in annual payments. This scale provides cost advantages (lower per-transaction cost), data advantages (fraud detection improves with more data), and negotiating leverage with card networks and banks. Scale is a reinforcing advantage - the larger Stripe gets, the better its economics and the harder it is for new entrants to match.

Trust through reliability: Payment processing is a trust business. Customers cannot tolerate downtime, errors, or fraud. Stripe has invested in reliability (99.999% uptime targets), security (PCI compliance, fraud detection), and global compliance (tax, regulatory, legal). This trust is socially complex - it takes years to build and cannot be acquired by imitators overnight.

The advantage statement explains WHY these three capabilities combine to win. It is not a list of features; it is a coherent story about why Stripe can capture this scope better than alternatives.

Why the 35-word format matters:

Collis and Rukstad argue that concise strategy statements are valuable for three reasons:

1. Alignment: A concise statement can be memorized and recited by every employee, enabling them to make decisions aligned with strategy without needing to look things up. Amazon's 'customer obsession' has guided thousands of decisions without constant reference to strategy documents.

2. Clarity: Writing a strategy in 35 words forces the author to make explicit choices about objective, scope, and advantage. Vague strategies cannot be compressed; only coherent strategies fit in 35 words.

3. Communication: Employees, investors, customers, and partners can understand a 35-word statement. Multi-page strategic plans are read by few people and understood by fewer.

The discipline of the format also prevents common strategy failures:
- Strategy without objective is a wish list.
- Strategy without scope is unfocused and tries to be everything.
- Strategy without advantage is just hope without a mechanism.

All three must be present, specific, and internally consistent.

Another example - Southwest Airlines: 'To provide affordable air travel to budget-conscious short-haul travelers within the United States, through efficient operations centered on a single aircraft type and point-to-point routes.' (27 words)

Other example - Patagonia: 'To save our home planet by inspiring environmental action, serving outdoor enthusiasts and committed environmentalists, through durable high-quality outdoor gear and activist brand leadership on environmental causes.' (29 words)

Test your own firm: can you write your firm's strategy in 35 words with clear objective, scope, and advantage? If not, the strategy likely is not coherent enough to execute.

rubric.key: Strategy statement with all three components in 35 words or fewer
Clear explanation of each component (objective, scope, advantage)
Application of Collis & Rukstad principles with why the format matters

Final presentation (20-25 minutes) to class and faculty panel

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected at all lectures and mandatory for case discussions and guest speaker sessions. More than 3 unexcused absences may reduce the participation grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without documented emergency.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of concepts and case approaches is encouraged. All individual assignments including case analyses, strategic diagnoses, and written deliverables must be your own work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted work must be your own. Copying case analyses or strategy designs from consulting reports, MBA case databases, or other students constitutes academic misconduct and will result in a grade of zero and reporting to the academic integrity office.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need Excel for financial analysis, access to company 10-K filings (EDGAR), industry data sources (IBISWorld, Statista), and the course textbooks. Lab computers are available.
